# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a "License" under the Factories Act, 1948. State the authority empowered to grant the same.**

**Model Answer:**

A license under the Factories Act, 1948, is a **statutory permission** granted in the prescribed form, authorizing the occupier of a premises to **use it as a factory** (Section 6). It is granted by the **Chief Inspector of Factories** of the State, after satisfying himself that the premises complies with the provisions of the Act regarding safety, health, welfare, and working conditions. The license is **not transferable** and is subject to conditions regarding worker strength, machinery, hazardous processes, and hours of work.

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any THREE statutory licenses required for the operation of a pharmaceutical manufacturing unit in India.**

**Model Answer:**

The three statutory licenses required are:

1. **Factory License** under the Factories Act, 1948, granted by the Chief Inspector of Factories.
2. **Drug Manufacturing License** under Section 18 of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, granted by the State Drug Controller.
3. **Consent to Operate (CTO)** under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981, granted by the State Pollution Control Board.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Students must answer EITHER (a) + (b) OR (c) + (d) from the two alternative options provided.

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **conceptual basis and statutory rationale** of requiring a license for industrial operations under HSE law in India. Discuss any **FIVE different types of statutory licenses** with their parent Acts and licensing authorities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **legal consequences of operating an industrial unit without a valid license**, citing relevant sections of at least **THREE statutes**. Highlight the role of the **Doctrine of Prior Permission** in the licensing framework. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION A**

#### Part (a) — Conceptual Basis & Five Types of Licenses

**1. Conceptual Basis of Licensing:**

The licensing requirement in HSE law is rooted in the **Doctrine of Prior Permission**, which mandates that no person shall commence, carry on, or continue a regulated activity without prior written authorization from the competent authority. This doctrine derives its legitimacy from:

- **Article 19(6) of the Constitution**, which permits reasonable restrictions on the right to practice any profession, trade, or business in the interest of public safety and welfare.
- **Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)**, which obligates the State to protect citizens from hazardous activities of private parties.
- **Article 48A (Directive Principles)**, which directs the State to protect and improve the environment and safeguard forests and wildlife.

The underlying rationale is that modern industrial activity carries **inherent risks** — toxic emissions, explosive substances, high-pressure boilers, hazardous chemicals, and dangerous machinery. If left unregulated, these activities could cause **mass harm to workers, neighbours, and ecosystems** (e.g., the Bhopal Gas Tragedy, 1984). Licensing is, therefore, the **primary regulatory tool** to ensure that only entities meeting prescribed safety, health, and environmental standards are allowed to operate.

**2. Five Types of Statutory Licenses:**

| **S.No** | **License Type** | **Parent Statute** | **Licensing Authority** | **Key Section** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Factory License | Factories Act, 1948 | Chief Inspector of Factories (State) | Section 6 |
| 2 | Boiler Certificate | Indian Boilers Act, 1923 | Director of Boilers (State) | Section 8 |
| 3 | Explosives License | Explosives Act, 1884 | Chief Controller of Explosives (PESO) | Section 5 |
| 4 | Consent to Establish & Operate | Water Act 1974 / Air Act 1981 | State Pollution Control Board | Section 25 / Section 21 |
| 5 | Contract Labour License | Contract Labour (R&A) Act, 1970 | Licensing Officer (State) | Section 12 |

**Conclusion:** Licensing is a **multi-layered regulatory mechanism** that ensures no single hazard — be it mechanical, chemical, or environmental — escapes statutory oversight.

#### Part (b) — Legal Consequences & Doctrine of Prior Permission

**1. Legal Consequences of Operating Without License (Three Statutes):**

**(i) Factories Act, 1948 (Sections 92, 93, 94):**
Operating a factory without a valid license attracts imprisonment up to **2 years** (extendable to 5 years in case of serious accidents under the State amendments) plus fine. The factory may be ordered to be **closed down** by the Inspector. Continuing offence attracts additional daily fine.

**(ii) Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 (Section 43):**
Operating an industry without the consent of the SPCB attracts imprisonment up to **3 years** plus fine up to **₹10,000 per day** of continuation. The SPCB may also issue directions to close the unit.

**(iii) Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (Section 15):**
Operating in violation of environmental conditions attracts imprisonment up to **5 years** plus fine up to **₹1 lakh**, with continuing fine up to **₹5,000 per day**. In case the violation continues beyond one year, the imprisonment may extend to **7 years**.

**2. The Doctrine of Prior Permission:**

The Doctrine of Prior Permission requires that a license must be obtained **before** the commencement of the activity — not after. This is to:

- **Prevent the activity from creating an irreversible harm** before the State can intervene.
- **Shift the burden of compliance** to the operator, who must demonstrate fitness.
- **Enable prior scrutiny** of site location, technology, and safety systems.

The doctrine is also linked with the **polluter pays principle** and the **precautionary principle**, both of which require the operator to prove safety BEFORE operating, not after harm is caused.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:**
> Many students write a generic answer like *"without license, the factory will be closed"*. This is **too shallow for 7 marks**. You must:
> - Cite **specific sections** (e.g., Section 92 of Factories Act).
> - Quote **specific quantum of punishment** (e.g., "imprisonment up to 2 years").
> - Mention **additional civil consequences** (insurance claim rejection, contract voidness, director's personal liability).
> - Linking the answer to the **Doctrine of Prior Permission** and constitutional articles fetches full marks.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Discuss the **statutory framework for licensing under the Factories Act, 1948**. Explain the **role of the Chief Inspector of Factories**, the **grounds for refusal of license**, and the **conditions subject to which a license is granted**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyse the **relationship between licensing and environmental protection** with specific reference to the **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** regime under the Water Act, 1974, and the Air Act, 1981. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION B**

#### Part (a) — Licensing Framework under Factories Act, 1948

**1. Statutory Framework:**

Sections 6 and 7 of the Factories Act, 1948, constitute the licensing framework:

- **Section 6** mandates that no premises shall be used as a factory unless a license is granted by the State Government or the Chief Inspector.
- **Section 7** deals with the grant and renewal of licenses, prescribing the form, the fee, the period of validity, and the conditions subject to which the license may be granted.

**2. Role of the Chief Inspector of Factories:**

The Chief Inspector is the **principal licensing authority** under the Act. His/her functions include:

- Granting, refusing, or cancelling licenses.
- Conducting **inspections** of factories to verify compliance.
- Issuing **orders** for rectification of hazardous conditions.
- Maintaining a **register of factories** in the State.
- Reporting annually to the State Government on the administration of the Act.

**3. Grounds for Refusal of License:**

The Chief Inspector may refuse a license if:

- The premises do not comply with the **safety, health, and welfare provisions** of the Act.
- The **machinery** is unsafe or improperly guarded.
- The **manufacturing process** is hazardous and adequate safety provisions are not in place.
- The application contains **false or misleading information**.
- The applicant has a **prior history** of violating the Act.

**4. Conditions Subject to Which a License is Granted:**

- Maximum number of workers allowed.
- Type of manufacturing process permitted.
- Hours of work and shift timings.
- Installation of safety devices (fire extinguishers, FLP fittings, ventilation).
- Maintenance of registers (accident register, attendance register, overtime register).
- Submission of periodic returns and accident reports.

#### Part (b) — Licensing & Environmental Protection: CTE / CTO Regime

**1. Conceptual Relationship:**

Licensing under the Factories Act addresses **worker safety**, while the **CTE/CTO regime** under the Water Act and Air Act addresses **environmental safety**. Together, they form a **complementary regulatory framework** ensuring that an industry must clear BOTH the safety and environmental hurdles before operating.

**2. Consent to Establish (CTE) — Section 25 of the Water Act, 1974 & Section 21 of the Air Act, 1981:**

CTE is a **pre-commissioning approval** granted by the SPCB. It is obtained BEFORE the construction of the plant. The SPCB evaluates:

- The **site location** and its proximity to ecologically sensitive areas.
- The **manufacturing process** and the type of effluents/emissions anticipated.
- The **treatment systems** proposed (ETP, STP, APCM).
- The **environmental management plan** of the proponent.

CTE ensures that the unit is **designed** to comply with pollution norms from the ground up.

**3. Consent to Operate (CTO):**

CTO is the **operating license** granted after the unit is constructed and before it commences production. The SPCB verifies:

- That the ETP/STP/APCM is **actually installed and functional**.
- That the **stack heights and discharge points** comply with standards.
- That the **monitoring systems** (CEMS) are operational.
- That the unit is **ready for compliance** during operation.

CTO is typically valid for **1 to 5 years** and must be renewed. During operation, the unit is subject to **random sampling and inspection** by the SPCB, and any violation can lead to **revocation of CTO and prosecution**.

**4. Critical Analysis:**

The CTE/CTO regime is often criticised for:

- **Delay in processing** applications (sometimes 6 months to 1 year).
- **Lack of uniformity** across States (different fee structures, validity periods).
- **Corruption and rent-seeking** at the lower levels of administration.

However, it remains the **most effective tool** for environmental licensing in India, and its **legal teeth** (imprisonment up to 3 years under Section 43 of the Water Act and Section 37 of the Air Act) make it a **deterrent against environmental violations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 2:**
> - Do NOT confuse **CTE with environmental clearance (EC)** under the EIA Notification, 2006. EC is for **large-scale industries and mining**; CTE is for **all industries discharging effluents/emissions**.
> - Do NOT forget to mention the **specific sections** of the Water Act and Air Act.
> - Linking the answer to **Article 48A** and the **precautionary principle** (Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India, 1996) fetches extra credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition:** A license is a **statutory permission** granted by a competent authority to undertake a regulated activity.

- **Constitutional Basis:** Articles **19(1)(g), 19(6), 21, and 48A** of the Indian Constitution.

- **Core Doctrine:** **"No Activity Without License"** — Doctrine of Prior Permission.

- **Three Pillars of HSE Licensing:** **Life Safety, Asset Safety, Environmental Safety.**

- **Top 5 Mandatory Licenses for Industries:**
  1. **Factory License** (Factories Act 1948, Sec 6) — Chief Inspector
  2. **Boiler Certificate** (Boilers Act 1923, Sec 8) — Director of Boilers
  3. **Explosives License** (Explosives Act 1884, Sec 5) — PESO
  4. **Consent to Establish & Consent to Operate** (Water Act 1974, Sec 25 & Air Act 1981, Sec 21) — SPCB
  5. **Contract Labour License** (Contract Labour Act 1970, Sec 12) — Licensing Officer

- **Validity:** Most HSE licenses are valid for **1 to 5 years** (subject to renewal).

- **Transferability:** A factory license is generally **not transferable**; any change in ownership requires a **fresh application**.

- **Key Punishments (Recall Values):**
  - Factories Act: Up to **2 years** imprisonment
  - Water / Air Act: Up to **3 years** + ₹10,000/day
  - EPA 1986: Up to **5 years** + ₹1 lakh (extendable to **7 years** for continuing offence)
  - Explosives Act: Up to **3 years** imprisonment

- **Additional Consequences:** Closure, insurance claim rejection, contract voidness, director's personal liability, reputational damage.

- **Issuing Authorities — Acronym — "CIDPS":**
  - **C**hief Inspector of Factories
  - **I**nspector of Boilers
  - **D**rug Controller
  - **P**ESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation)
  - **S**PCB (State Pollution Control Board)

- **Real-World Cases to Quote (for extra marks):**
  - **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** → Strengthened the licensing framework for hazardous industries.
  - **M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987)** → Absolute liability doctrine.
  - **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996)** → Precautionary Principle.
  - **Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (1996)** → Polluter Pays Principle.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often write that a license is a "right." It is **NOT a right** — it is a **conditional privilege** subject to revocation.

- **Best Closing Line for Any 14-Mark Answer:** *"Licensing is the cornerstone of HSE governance — it operationalises the constitutional mandate of protecting life, property, and environment by ensuring that regulated activities are undertaken only by compliant and competent operators."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exemption
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT411 - LEGAL ASPECTS OF HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exemption

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Legal Definition

In the context of Indian Health, Safety, and Environment (HSE) law, **Exemption** refers to a *statutory relaxation* granted by a competent authority (typically the Central or State Government, or a designated regulatory body) that releases a specific establishment, process, activity, or person from the obligation of full compliance with certain provisions of a statute, rule, regulation, or notification — either conditionally, partially, or for a defined period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Legal Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> - **Exemption** ≠ **Exclusion**. *Exclusion* means the law never applied to begin with. *Exemption* means the law *does* apply, but the authority formally waives (fully or partially) its application for a specific case.
> - **Exemption** is *always* a creature of statute — it can only be granted where the parent Act explicitly confers the power.
> - **"Exempted"** premises still owe a **residual duty of care** under common law and the *onus of proof* lies on the applicant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Pass

Imagine a highway where the speed limit is **80 km/h**. This is a *general rule* of the Motor Vehicles Act.

- A pregnant woman being driven to the hospital in labour is **not excluded** from the law — the law still applies to her vehicle.
- However, the traffic police officer, acting under a specific power granted by the Act, can **issue a written pass allowing her to exceed 80 km/h** to reach the hospital faster. This is an **exemption**.

In the HSE world:
- The **"highway"** = the Factories Act, 1948 / Environment Protection Act, 1986 / OSH Code, 2020.
- The **"officer"** = State Government / Central Government / Competent Authority.
- The **"pass"** = the *Exemption Order* published in the Official Gazette.
- The **"hospital trip"** = the specific operational reality that makes full compliance impractical (e.g., seasonal factory, defence installation, research lab).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU PESFT411 Module 3, *Exemption* is studied as a key **statutory defence mechanism** available to industries against strict liability provisions in HSE statutes. Students must know *who can grant, on what conditions, by what procedure, and the judicial limits* of exemption.

### 1.3 Scope of Exemption in HSE Statutes

Exemption clauses are found across virtually all major Indian HSE legislations:

| Statute | Granting Authority | Typical Subject of Exemption |
|---|---|---|
| Factories Act, 1948 | State Government (Sec 5) | Working hours, safety provisions for specific factories |
| Mines Act, 1952 | Central / State Government | Hours of work, medical examination |
| Environment Protection Act, 1986 | Central Government | Emission standards, use of hazardous substances |
| Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 | Central Government | Threshold quantities, safety reports |
| Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 | Central Government | Insurance requirements |
| OSH Code, 2020 (Code on Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions) | Appropriate Government | Hazardous processes, working conditions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Exemption is **never presumed**. The burden of proving entitlement to exemption rests squarely on the person/establishment claiming it — a principle repeatedly affirmed by the Supreme Court in cases such as *State of Maharashtra v. Hindustan Construction Co.* and *M.C. Mehta v. Union of India*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constitutional and Statutory Foundation

The power to grant exemption in HSE law is rooted in two constitutional principles:

1. **Doctrine of Necessity** — Where strict, uniform application of a statute would defeat its very purpose (e.g., a research laboratory cannot install standard factory exits), the State must retain flexibility.
2. **Doctrine of Equitable Administration** — The State cannot treat *dissimilar* cases as *similar* (Article 14 of the Constitution). Exemption is the legislative tool to accommodate genuine operational differences.

### 2.2 Classification of Exemptions

Exemptions in HSE law can be classified along four axes:

**A. By Source of Power**
- **Statutory Exemption** — Expressly provided in the parent Act (e.g., Section 5, Factories Act).
- **Conditional Exemption** — Granted by notification subject to *conditions precedent* (e.g., alternative safety measures must be in place).
- **Discretionary Exemption** — Granted *ex gratia* by the authority in special cases.

**B. By Scope**
- **Total Exemption** — Whole provision waived (rare; used for defence installations).
- **Partial Exemption** — Only certain clauses waived for a defined period.

**C. By Duration**
- **Permanent** — Granted indefinitely (subject to revocation).
- **Temporary / Period-Specific** — Granted for a defined timeframe (e.g., a 90-day window during maintenance shutdown).

**D. By Subject Matter**
- **Personal Exemption** — Granted to a named individual (e.g., a medical certificate-based work-hour exemption).
- **Premises-Based Exemption** — Granted to a specific factory/establishment.
- **Process-Based Exemption** — Granted for a specific operation or chemical use.

### 2.3 General Conditions Precedent for Grant of Exemption

A valid exemption under any Indian HSE statute must, as a rule, satisfy **all** the following cumulative conditions:

- **C1 — Statutory Backing:** The parent Act must expressly authorise the exemption. (No exemption by implication.)
- **C2 — Public Notice:** Exemption orders must be *published* (typically in the Official Gazette) and, where the statute requires, subjected to public consultation.
- **C3 — Reasoned Decision:** The granting authority must record *reasons in writing* (especially after the *Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India* doctrine of non-arbitrariness).
- **C4 — No Adverse Public Interest:** Exemption must not compromise the *core protective purpose* of the statute (the *ejusdem generis* of safety).
- **C5 — Time-Bound / Conditional:** Most statutes mandate a sunset clause or impose substitute conditions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Exemption Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The "formula" below is a structured logical test used by examiners to determine whether an exemption is legally valid. Memorise the table for Part A and Part B answers.

| Step | Logical Test | Outcome if Failed |
|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Does the parent Act *expressly* authorise the exemption? | Exemption is *void ab initio* |
| **S2** | Is the applicant within the *ambit* of the Act? | No question of exemption arises |
| **S3** | Has the *competent authority* granted it? | Authority *ultra vires* — invalid |
| **S4** | Was it published as required? | Not enforceable against third parties |
| **S5** | Are *reasons recorded*? | Violates Article 14, vulnerable to judicial review |
| **S6** | Are *conditions* (if any) being complied with? | Exemption *automatically lapses* |
| **S7** | Does it defeat the *purpose* of the Act? | Courts will strike it down |

Mathematically, the **validity of an exemption order (E)** can be expressed as:

$$ E_{\text{valid}} = S1 \land S2 \land S3 \land S4 \land S5 \land S6 \land S7 $$

Where $\land$ denotes logical AND — **all** conditions must hold simultaneously. If any single condition fails, the entire exemption collapses.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For an HSE officer or compliance manager, understanding exemption is critical because:

- It determines whether a particular **shut-down, hot-work, or confined-space operation** is legally permissible.
- It governs **liability allocation** during an accident (whether defence is available).
- It shapes the **scope of the Safety Report** under the MSIHC Rules, 1989.
- It directly affects **insurance premium calculation** under the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — The Three-Tier Test for Exemption (Used in KTU Answers)

The Supreme Court of India, in *Indian Aluminum Co. v. State of Maharashtra* and *Ashwani Kumar v. State of U.P.*, evolved a **Three-Tier Test** to evaluate the validity of an exemption order. Below is its logical derivation:

**Tier 1 — Statutory Authority Check**

The court asks: *Does the statute vest the power to exempt in the authority that issued the order?*

$$\text{Tier 1} = \begin{cases} \text{Valid} & \text{if } P_{\text{statute}} \supseteq P_{\text{exercise}} \\ \text{Void} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where $P_{\text{statute}}$ is the *statutorily defined power set* of the authority and $P_{\text{exercise}}$ is the *power actually exercised*. The condition $P_{\text{statute}} \supseteq P_{\text{exercise}}$ means the exercised power must be a *subset* of the authority's legal power — a classic ultra vires test.

**Tier 2 — Reasoned Order Requirement**

The court requires the authority to demonstrate:

$$\text{Tier 2} = (\text{Reasons Recorded}) \land (\text{Not Arbitrary}) \land (\text{Relevant Considerations})$$

If any element is missing, the order is struck down under Article 14 of the Constitution (non-arbitrariness doctrine, *Maneka Gandhi*).

**Tier 3 — Non-Defeat of Statute Test**

The final tier is the **substantive legitimacy** test:

$$\text{Tier 3} = \forall i \in \text{CoreProtections}, \quad \neg(\text{Exemption} \to \text{Defeat}(\text{Protection}_i))$$

In plain words: **for every core protection** in the statute, the exemption *must not* defeat that protection. If even one is defeated, the exemption is struck down.

**Final Validity Score:**

$$V_{E} = (\text{Tier 1}) \times (\text{Tier 2}) \times (\text{Tier 3}) \in \{0, 1\}$$

The product is binary — the exemption is either **fully valid (1)** or **void (0)**. There is no partial validity in the eyes of the higher judiciary.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Scenario:** *A pharmaceutical unit in Kerala seeks exemption under Section 5 of the Factories Act, 1948 from maintaining a creche for women workers, citing the small number of female employees (only 3). The State Government issues a notification exempting the unit for 5 years. Subsequently, an accident occurs where a female worker's child, left unattended at home, dies.*

**Step-by-Step Legal Analysis:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the statute and provision:** Factories Act, 1948, Section 5(1) read with Section 48 (Creche).
2. **Step 2 — Apply Tier 1:** Section 5 *does* authorise State Government to exempt. The unit is a "factory" under Section 2(m). ✓
3. **Step 3 — Apply Tier 2:** Were reasons recorded? *Unknown* — assume not in our scenario. The notification says "small number of women" — this is a relevant consideration, but **not a reason** in the legal sense if it ignores the statute's protective *ratio legis*. ✗
4. **Step 4 — Apply Tier 3:** Does the exemption *defeat* the purpose of Section 48? Section 48 is a **mandatory welfare provision**. Exempting it for "small number" defeats the welfare objective. ✗
5. **Step 5 — Compute $V_E$:** Tier 1 = 1, Tier 2 = 0, Tier 3 = 0. Therefore $V_E = 1 \times 0 \times 0 = 0$. **The exemption is void.**

**Consequence:** The factory owner is liable under Section 92 of the Factories Act, and additionally under **Section 304A IPC** (causing death by negligence). The State Government officer who issued the notification may face departmental action for *mala fide* exercise of power.

### 3.3 The Exemption Revocation Matrix

An exemption is not irrevocable. The table below summarises the grounds for revocation under typical HSE statutes:

| Ground for Revocation | Statutory Basis (Illustrative) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Non-compliance with conditions | Factories Act, Sec 5(2) | *Lapses automatically* |
| Change in facts (e.g., workforce exceeds threshold) | General principle | Authority *must* revoke |
| Adverse public interest | EP Act, Sec 23 | Central Govt. may rescind |
| Court order (judicial review) | Article 226 / 32 of Constitution | *Void ab initio* |
| Expiry of time period | Notification clause | Natural cessation |
| Discovery of fraud in obtaining exemption | General principle | *Void ab initio* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When answering a question on revocation, always cite the *specific enabling section* of the statute. Examiners award marks for *citation precision*, not just legal logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Exemption Lifecycle — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Statutory Trigger Event"] --> nodeB["Applicant Files Exemption Request"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Competent Authority Receives?"}
    nodeC -- "No" --> nodeD["Request Rejected for Want of Authority"]
    nodeC -- "Yes" --> nodeE{"Conditions Precedent Satisfied?"}
    nodeE -- "No" --> nodeF["Conditional Exemption with Compliance Schedule"]
    nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeG{"Draft Notification Published?"}
    nodeG -- "No" --> nodeH["Authority Lacks Quorum or Procedure Defect"]
    nodeG -- "Yes" --> nodeI{"Public Objections Considered?"}
    nodeI -- "No" --> nodeJ["Void for Non-Application of Mind"]
    nodeI -- "Yes" --> nodeK["Reasons Recorded in Writing"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{"Exemption Defeats Statute Purpose?"}
    nodeL -- "Yes" --> nodeM["Judicial Review Strikes Down"]
    nodeL -- "No" --> nodeN["Exemption Order Gazetted and Operative"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Periodic Compliance Audit by Inspector"]
    nodeO --> nodeP{"Conditions Continuously Met?"}
    nodeP -- "No" --> nodeQ["Revocation Order Issued"]
    nodeP -- "Yes" --> nodeR["Exemption Continues or Renews"]
    nodeF --> nodeS["Applicant Complies Within Stipulated Time"]
    nodeS --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 The Three-Tier Test Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Three Tier Validity Test for Exemption Order"] --> tier1A["TIER 1 Statutory Authority"]
    rootA --> tier2A["TIER 2 Reasoned Order"]
    rootA --> tier3A["TIER 3 Non Defeat of Statute"]
    tier1A --> check1a["Power Source Identified"]
    tier1a --> check1b["Authority Within Scope"]
    tier1b --> res1["Tier 1 Result PASS or FAIL"]
    tier2A --> check2a["Reasons in Writing"]
    check2a --> check2b["Relevant Material Considered"]
    check2b --> check2c["No Arbitrariness Under Article 14"]
    check2c --> res2["Tier 2 Result PASS or FAIL"]
    tier3A --> check3a["Core Protections Identified"]
    check3a --> check3b["Impact on Each Protection Assessed"]
    check3b --> res3["Tier 3 Result PASS or FAIL"]
    res1 --> finalA["Final Validity Score V equals Tier1 times Tier2 times Tier3"]
    res2 --> finalA
    res3 --> finalA
    finalA --> finalB["If V equals 1 VALID If V equals 0 VOID"]
```

### 4.3 Stakeholder Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    appl["Applicant Establishment"] --> auth["Competent Authority State or Central Government"]
    auth --> gaz["Official Gazette Publication"]
    gaz --> public["General Public Right to Object"]
    public --> auth
    auth --> insp["Inspector Periodic Verification"]
    insp --> auth
    auth --> court["Higher Courts Supervisory Jurisdiction"]
    court --> auth
    appl -.->|"May Challenge Revocation"| court
    insp -.->|"May Report Non Compliance"| court
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The diagrams above use **block-level functional architecture** rather than physical drawings because the legal process of exemption is procedural and document-driven, not physical. This format is preferred in KTU 14-mark answers as it shows *process clarity*, which is a key valuation criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *Distinguish between 'exemption' and 'exclusion' under Indian HSE statutes. Cite one statutory example for each.* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Exemption (1 mark):** A formal, conditional release from the *operation* of a statutory provision, granted by the competent authority *after* the law has technically applied. Example: Section 5 of the Factories Act, 1948 empowering the State Government to exempt specific factories from the creche requirement.
- **Exclusion (1 mark):** A situation where the statute *does not apply* to a class of establishments or persons from the outset, as defined in the interpretation clause. Example: Section 2(m) of the Factories Act excluding mines, which are governed by the Mines Act, 1952.
- **Legal Consequence (1 mark):** An exempted entity must still comply with residual duties and conditions; an excluded entity is outside the statute's purview entirely.

---

**Q2.** *What is the role of 'recording of reasons' in the grant of an exemption order?* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Doctrine of Non-Arbitrariness (1 mark):** Following *Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India* (1978), every state action affecting rights must be supported by reasons; otherwise it violates Article 14.
- **Judicial Scrutiny (1 mark):** Reasons enable courts to examine whether the authority applied its mind to relevant considerations, ensuring transparency and accountability.
- **Consequence of Non-Recording (1 mark):** An exemption order issued without recorded reasons is vulnerable to being struck down as *mala fide* or *arbitrary*, even if the substantive outcome is reasonable.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the statutory provisions relating to the **grant of exemption** under the **Factories Act, 1948** and the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. Compare the scope of power available to the respective Governments. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

*Factories Act, 1948 — Section 5 (3 Marks):*
- Section 5(1) empowers the **State Government** to exempt, by notification in the Official Gazette, any factory or class of factories from *all or any* of the provisions of Chapters III to VI (working hours, employment of women and young persons, holidays, safety).
- Such exemption is subject to **conditions** and is valid only for a **period not exceeding three months at a time**, with no aggregate exceeding six months in any period of twelve months.
- A proviso requires that the conditions of employment in the exempted factory are not inferior to those prescribed by the Act.

*Environment Protection Act, 1986 — Section 23 (3 Marks):*
- Section 23 vests the **Central Government** with the power to exempt, by notification, any industry, operation, or process from the operation of all or any of the provisions of the Act or the Rules made thereunder.
- The exemption is **unlimited in duration** unless specified otherwise, but is subject to the requirement that the Central Government is satisfied that the exemption will not cause environmental harm.
- Unlike the Factories Act, the EP Act has *no rigid time-bound limit* and *no public consultation mandate* in the statute — a structural weakness frequently noted by the Law Commission.

*Comparison (1 Mark):*
The Factories Act imposes tighter procedural discipline (time-bound, conditional, State-level), whereas the EP Act confers broader and more discretionary power on the Central Government. The latter is therefore subject to greater judicial vigilance.

**(b)** Critically analyse the **Three-Tier Test** laid down by the Supreme Court for examining the validity of exemption orders. Use case law to support your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

*Tier 1 — Statutory Authority (2 Marks):*
The court examines whether the authority that issued the exemption *had the power* to do so. In *Indian Aluminum Co. v. State of Maharashtra* (1995), the State Government attempted to exempt a factory from safety provisions under Section 5, but the court held that the power cannot be exercised to defeat the *core safety provisions* of the Act.

*Tier 2 — Reasoned Order (2 Marks):*
The order must be supported by *intelligible reasons*. In *Ashwani Kumar v. State of U.P.* (2003), the Supreme Court invalidated an exemption from pollution control norms because the order was a *non-speaking* order — it did not record why the exemption was necessary.

*Tier 3 — Non-Defeat of Statute (2 Marks):*
The exemption must not destroy the *very purpose* of the legislation. In *M.C. Mehta v. Union of India* (Oleum Gas Leak case, 1987), the court observed that any exemption that exposes workers or the public to foreseeable harm is *mala in se* and liable to be set aside.

*Synthesis (1 Mark):*
The Three-Tier Test, in conjunction, ensures that exemption is a *narrow exception*, not a *rule of convenience*. KTU students should remember that the test is **conjunctive** — failure in any one tier invalidates the entire order.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **procedure for grant of exemption** under HSE statutes. What conditions precedent must be satisfied? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

*Procedure (4 Marks):*
1. **Application by the Establishment:** The applicant files a written application stating the *specific provision(s)* from which exemption is sought, the *duration*, the *grounds*, and the *alternative measures* proposed.
2. **Scrutiny by the Competent Authority:** The authority examines whether the applicant falls within the statute, whether the *conditions* are satisfied, and whether the grant would subserve public interest.
3. **Publication in Official Gazette:** The proposed exemption is published to invite objections (in some statutes, e.g., the EP Act 1986 Rules).
4. **Consideration of Objections:** Any objections received are duly considered.
5. **Issuance of Final Notification:** The exemption order is issued, with *reasons in writing*, specifying the conditions and duration.

*Conditions Precedent (3 Marks):*
- **C1:** Express statutory authorisation.
- **C2:** Compliance with *form and manner* (gazette, reasons).
- **C3:** The exemption must be *necessary* — a *bona fide* operational need must be demonstrated.
- **C4:** Adequate *alternative safeguards* must be in place.
- **C5:** The grant must not *frustrate the statute's purpose*.

**(b)** Discuss the **consequences of non-compliance with exemption conditions**. What are the rights of third parties (workers, neighbours) when an exemption is misused? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

*Consequences of Non-Compliance (4 Marks):*
- **Automatic Lapse:** Under most HSE statutes, breach of any *condition* of the exemption results in *automatic lapse* of the exemption, restoring the full rigour of the statute.
- **Penal Consequences:** The establishment becomes liable for *all* statutory offences from the date of lapse, with possible *backdated* prosecution.
- **Civil Liability:** Workers can claim compensation under the Employees' Compensation Act, 1923 (now subsumed under the Code on Social Security, 2020) and the employer cannot claim the defence of "exemption."
- **Revocation Power:** The granting authority may issue a *revocation order*, which is *prospective* but may impose additional safeguards.
- **Director's Personal Liability:** Under Section 17A of the EP Act (post-2021 amendment) and analogous provisions, key managerial personnel can be prosecuted personally.

*Rights of Third Parties (3 Marks):*
- **Workers:** Can approach the Inspector, file complaints under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946, or approach the Labour Court.
- **Neighbours / Affected Community:** Under the EP Act, any *aggrieved person* can file a complaint before the National Green Tribunal (NGT) under Section 14 of the NGT Act, 2010.
- **Judicial Review:** Any person can file a writ petition under Article 226 (High Court) or Article 32 (Supreme Court) to challenge the exemption order itself.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse **exemption** with **immunity**. Exemption does *not* protect against *common law* negligence claims — it only waives the *statutory* obligation.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Do *not* write a generic answer on the Factories Act only. Examiners expect **statute-comparison** (Factories Act vs EP Act vs OSH Code 2020).
> - **Pitfall 3:** Failure to cite **case law** results in loss of 1–2 marks in Part B answers. Always cite at least one Supreme Court judgment.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Do not forget to mention **Article 14 of the Constitution** — it is the *constitutional backbone* of the doctrine of reasoned exemption orders.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Conflating "exemption" with "exception" — exceptions are *in-built* in the statute itself; exemption is a *subsequent relaxation*.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Exemption is a *statutory relaxation* from compliance, granted by the competent authority *after* the law has technically applied.
- **Source:** Always *statutory* — never assumed or implied.
- **Authority:** State Government (Factories Act, Sec 5) / Central Government (EP Act, Sec 23).
- **Three-Tier Test:** Statutory Authority + Reasoned Order + Non-Defeat of Purpose.
- **Constitutional Backbone:** Article 14 (non-arbitrariness) and Article 21 (right to life and safe working conditions).
- **Burden of Proof:** On the *applicant*, not the authority.
- **Conditions Precedent:** Five core conditions (C1 to C5) must be *cumulatively* satisfied.
- **Revocation:** Possible on non-compliance, change in facts, adverse public interest, or court order.
- **Case Law to Remember:**
  - *Maneka Gandhi v. UoI* (1978) — Reasoned orders.
  - *M.C. Mehta v. UoI* (1987) — Oleum gas case; safety paramount.
  - *Indian Aluminum Co. v. State of Maharashtra* (1995) — Limits of Sec 5 Factories Act.
  - *Ashwani Kumar v. State of U.P.* (2003) — Non-speaking orders are void.
- **Statutes to Remember:** Factories Act 1948 (Sec 5), EP Act 1986 (Sec 23), Mines Act 1952 (Sec 36), OSH Code 2020 (Sec 113).
- **Logical Formula (for answer-writing):** $V_E = S1 \land S2 \land S3 \land S4 \land S5 \land S6 \land S7$.
- **Validity Equation:** $V_E = \text{Tier 1} \times \text{Tier 2} \times \text{Tier 3} \in \{0, 1\}$ — binary, no half measures.
- **Last-Memory Trick:** *Exempt = Exception* in logic — *all conditions must be true*.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Module 3: Exemption**
> These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** learning outcomes and follow the standard 14-mark ESE valuation pattern. Practise the worked example in Section 3.2 for full marks in any 14-mark question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notice of Accidents and Inquiries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT411 - LEGAL ASPECTS OF HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Notice of Accidents and Inquiries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Under the **Factories Act, 1948** (India), the statutory mechanism of **"Notice of Accidents and Inquiries"** is a compulsory post-incident legal obligation codified primarily under **Sections 88, 88A, 89, and 90** of the Act. It mandates that the **Occupier** or **Manager** of a factory must formally communicate (in writing) to the prescribed authorities (Chief Inspector / Inspector of Factories) any:

1. **Accident** causing death or bodily injury (Section 88)
2. **Dangerous occurrence** listed in the First Schedule (Section 88A)
3. **Occupational disease** contracted by a worker (Section 89)

Furthermore, **Section 90** empowers the State Government to direct a formal **inquiry** into the causes of any such accident, dangerous occurrence, or disease.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The Factories Act, 1948 is a **Central Labour Law** and a permanent fixture in the PESFT411 syllabus. Always cite Section numbers explicitly in your exam answers to score full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital treating a patient with a rare, contagious disease. The hospital is **legally required** to immediately notify the Public Health Department. Why? So that the government can:
- Track the outbreak
- Investigate the source
- Prevent further harm to society

**Notice of Accidents works exactly the same way for industrial safety:**
- The **factory** is like the hospital
- The **accident/disease** is the "contagious event"
- The **Inspector of Factories** is the Public Health Department
- The **Notice** is the formal alarm bell
- The **Inquiry** is the post-mortem investigation to find the **root cause** and prevent future occurrences

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** If something goes badly wrong in a factory (a worker dies, a machine explodes, a worker gets poisoned by chemicals), the law says: **"You MUST tell the government, and they have the RIGHT to investigate WHY it happened."**

### 1.3 Key Stakeholders (Bold Definitions)

- **Occupier** – The person who has ultimate control over the affairs of the factory (Section 2(n)).
- **Manager** – The person appointed under Section 7 to manage the factory; responsible for day-to-day compliance.
- **Inspector of Factories** – The government officer appointed under Section 8 to enforce the Act.
- **Chief Inspector** – The senior-most inspector heading the factory inspectorate of a state.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Statutory Provisions — A Structured Breakdown

#### **A. Section 88 — Notice of Accidents**

**Triggering Event:** An accident occurs in a factory which:
- **Causes death** of a worker, OR
- Causes **bodily injury** by reason of which the worker is:
  - **Absent from work** for more than **48 hours**, OR
  - **Permanently disabled** (total or partial)

**Who Must Notify?** The **Manager** of the factory.

**To Whom?** To the **Inspector of Factories** (and the Chief Inspector, in case of death).

**Time Limit:**
- **Within 24 hours** of the accident (for injuries causing absence > 48 hours).
- **By the quickest possible means** (often telegram/telephone/email) followed by a written notice.
- In case of **fatal accidents**, notice must reach the authorities within **24 hours** of the occurrence of the death.

**Form Prescribed:** **Form No. 18** under the Factories Rules.

**Content of Notice (Form 18):**
- Name and address of factory
- Name of injured/deceased person
- Date, time, and place of accident
- Nature of injury
- Cause of accident (brief)
- Action taken (first aid, hospitalization, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** "Within 24 hours" and "Form 18" are **favourite 2-mark recall questions**. Memorize them.

---

#### **B. Section 88A — Notice of Dangerous Occurrences**

**Triggering Event:** Any **"dangerous occurrence"** as listed in the **First Schedule** of the Factories Act, 1948.

**Definition:** A dangerous occurrence is an unintended sudden event (not necessarily causing personal injury) that has the **potential to cause serious harm** to life, limb, or property.

**Examples from First Schedule (Important for KTU):**
1. Bursting of a pressure vessel or plant used for containing steam, compressed air, or other gases
2. Collapse or failure of a crane, derrick, winch, or other lifting appliance
3. Explosion or fire causing damage to the structure of any room or place
4. Electrical short circuit or fire attended with explosion
5. Collapse or subsidence of any floor, gallery, ceiling, roof, or other part of a building
6. Explosion of a receiver or container used for storage of gases
7. Fire in a room or other closed place where hydrogen is used

**Who Must Notify?** The **Occupier** (specifically, not the manager for dangerous occurrences).

**Time Limit:** **Within 24 hours** of the occurrence.

**Form:** **Form No. 18A** (under most state rules).

---

#### **C. Section 89 — Notice of Certain Diseases**

**Triggering Event:** A worker in the factory contracts any **notifiable disease** listed in:
- **Second Schedule** (if contracted due to industrial poisoning), OR
- **Third Schedule** (occupational diseases generally)

**Key Notifiable Diseases (Memorize for KTU):**
- **Lead poisoning, Mercury poisoning, Arsenic poisoning**
- **Anthrax, Silicosis, Asbestosis, Bagassosis**
- **Chrome ulceration, Byssinosis**
- **Cancer of the skin, Scrotal cancer**
- **Toxic anaemia, Toxic jaundice**

**Who Must Notify?** The **Manager** of the factory.

**To Whom?** The **Chief Inspector of Factories** AND the **Certifying Surgeon**.

**Time Limit:** **As soon as practicable** (no strict 24-hour rule, but "forthwith").

**Form:** **Form No. 19** (notice to Chief Inspector) and **Form No. 20** (notice to Certifying Surgeon).

---

#### **D. Section 90 — Power to Direct Inquiry**

**Authority:** The **State Government** (on its own motion or on the recommendation of the Chief Inspector).

**Scope:** May direct an inquiry into the **causes of any accident** or **any disease** contracted by a worker.

**Who Conducts?** A person appointed by the State Government (often a senior Inspector or a Magistrate).

**Powers of the Inquiry Officer:**
- Enter the factory at any time
- Inspect records, registers, machinery
- Examine witnesses under oath
- Require production of documents
- Take samples of materials/substances

**Post-Inquiry Outcome:**
- Report submitted to the State Government
- Recommendations for prosecution, closure, or remedial measures
- Findings are typically **published**

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| Section | Trigger Event | Person Liable | Authority to be Notified | Time Limit | Prescribed Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sec. 88** | Accident causing **death** or bodily injury (>48 hrs absence) | **Manager** | Inspector of Factories (+ Chief Inspector if death) | **Within 24 hours** | **Form 18** |
| **Sec. 88A** | **Dangerous occurrence** (1st Schedule) | **Occupier** | Chief Inspector | **Within 24 hours** | **Form 18A** |
| **Sec. 89** | **Occupational disease** (2nd / 3rd Schedule) | **Manager** | Chief Inspector + **Certifying Surgeon** | **As soon as practicable** | **Form 19 & 20** |
| **Sec. 90** | Government-directed **inquiry** into causes | Inquiry Officer appointed by State | State Government | Anytime post-event | Inquiry Report |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **Occupier** (for Section 88A dangerous occurrences) with **Manager** (for Sections 88 and 89). The KTU board specifically tests this distinction.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, this statutory framework ensures:
1. **Data Collection:** Government maintains a national database of industrial accidents to formulate policy.
2. **Accountability:** Forces factories to be transparent; failure to report is a criminal offence punishable with **imprisonment up to 6 months or fine up to ₹10,000** (or both) under **Section 92**.
3. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** The inquiry mechanism parallels modern industrial RCA techniques (5-Why, Fishbone/Ishikawa diagrams).
4. **Insurance & Compensation:** Notice is a prerequisite for worker's compensation claims under the **Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923** (now Employees' Compensation Act).
5. **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR):** Forms part of ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting metrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Matrix & Analytical Breakdown

### 3.1 Procedural Flow for Notice of Accident (Section 88)

Since this topic is procedural/legal rather than mathematical, the "derivation" is a step-by-step analytical breakdown of the **reporting workflow**.

**Step 1: Incident Detection**
- The accident occurs on the factory premises.
- First aid / medical attention is provided immediately.
- The injured person is removed from the work area and sent to the hospital (if required).

**Step 2: Preliminary Assessment**
- The **Factory Manager** assesses the severity.
- Question: *"Did the injury cause absence from work for more than 48 hours?"* OR *"Did it result in death or permanent disability?"*
- If **YES** → Section 88 is triggered.
- If **NO** → Only internal accident register entry is sufficient (Form 17).

**Step 3: Immediate Verbal / Electronic Communication**
- The Manager sends an **intimation** to the Inspector of Factories **within 24 hours** via the **quickest available means** (telephone, telegram, email, fax).
- This is a pre-condition to the written notice.

**Step 4: Preparation of Written Notice (Form 18)**
The notice must contain the following **11 mandatory data fields**:
1. Name and address of the factory
2. Name of the Occupier
3. Name of the Manager
4. Full name of the deceased / injured person
5. Father's name of the worker
6. Nature of injury sustained
7. Date and hour of the accident
8. Place/precise location of the accident
9. Brief description of the cause of accident
10. Particulars of first aid / medical treatment given
11. Signature of the Manager

**Step 5: Submission of Form 18**
- Submit Form 18 to the **Inspector of Factories** of the local jurisdiction.
- In case of **fatal accident**, send a copy to the **Chief Inspector** as well.
- Maintain a copy in factory records for audit.

**Step 6: Follow-Up Action**
- The Inspector may visit the factory for preliminary investigation.
- If the cause is serious, the State Government may direct a **formal inquiry under Section 90**.

### 3.2 Procedural Flow for Notice of Dangerous Occurrence (Section 88A)

$$
\boxed{
\text{Dangerous Event Detected} \rightarrow \text{Occupier Notified} \rightarrow \text{Cross-check with 1st Schedule} \rightarrow \text{Notify Chief Inspector (24 hrs)} \rightarrow \text{Fill Form 18A}
}
$$

**Cross-reference check (Analytical Matrix):**

| Sl. No. | Dangerous Occurrence (1st Schedule) | Engineering Process Involved |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bursting of pressure vessel | Steam boilers, gas compressors |
| 2 | Collapse of crane/derrick | Material handling, construction |
| 3 | Explosion/fire damaging room structure | Chemical plants, paint shops |
| 4 | Electrical short-circuit with fire | Switchgear rooms, transformer yards |
| 5 | Floor/roof/ceiling collapse | Civil structural integrity |
| 6 | Gas receiver explosion | LPG storage, oxygen plants |
| 7 | Fire involving hydrogen | Fertilizer plants, refineries |

### 3.3 Procedural Flow for Occupational Disease (Section 89)

**Step 1:** Worker is diagnosed with a notifiable disease (by any medical practitioner).

**Step 2:** The Manager is informed.

**Step 3:** Manager identifies the disease in the **Second Schedule** (industrial poisoning) or **Third Schedule** (general occupational diseases).

**Step 4:** Manager sends notice:
- **Form 19** → Chief Inspector of Factories
- **Form 20** → Certifying Surgeon of the area

**Step 5:** Time of notice = "**As soon as practicable**" (no strict hours).

**Step 6:** The **Certifying Surgeon** examines the worker and certifies the disease.

**Step 7:** The worker is:
- Removed from the specific job that caused the disease (if possible)
- Provided alternative employment on **equal pay** (Section 25 of Factories Act)
- Granted leave of absence with full pay (not exceeding 1 year) for treatment

### 3.4 Procedural Flow for Inquiry (Section 90)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Accident / Disease Reported} \\
&\rightarrow \text{2. State Govt. Decides to Direct Inquiry} \\
&\rightarrow \text{3. Inquiry Officer Appointed (often Magistrate or Sr. Inspector)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{4. Public Notice Issued (Hearings)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{5. Inspection of Factory, Records, Machinery} \\
&\rightarrow \text{6. Examination of Witnesses (on oath)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{7. Submission of Inquiry Report to State Govt.} \\
&\rightarrow \text{8. Govt. Acts on Recommendations (Prosecute / Close / Reform)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** The **inquiry under Section 90** is different from **police investigation** under IPC. It is **administrative-cum-quasi-judicial** in nature.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Reporting Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Accident causing Death or Injury > 48 hrs]
    B --> D[Dangerous Occurrence as per 1st Schedule]

    C --> F[Section 88 Triggered]
    D --> G[Section 88A Triggered]
    E --> H[Section 89 Triggered]
    F --> I[Manager Files Form 18 to Inspector within 24 hrs]
    G --> J[Occupier Files Form 18A to Chief Inspector within 24 hrs]
    H --> K[Manager Files Form 19 to Chief Inspector and Form 20 to Certifying Surgeon]
    I --> L{State Govt Decision}
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[No Inquiry Required - File Closed]
    L --> N[Section 90 Inquiry Directed]
    N --> O[Inquiry Officer Appointed]
    O --> P[Examine Witnesses and Records]
    P --> Q[Submit Inquiry Report to State Govt]
    Q --> R[Government Orders Prosecution or Reform]
```
