## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the provisions regarding notice of accidents as laid down in Section 88 of the Factories Act, 1948.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per **Section 88** of the Factories Act, 1948:
1. Where in any factory an accident occurs which causes the **death** of any worker, or which causes **bodily injury** by reason of which the worker is **absent from work for more than 48 hours**, the **Manager of the factory** shall forthwith send a notice thereof to the **Inspector of Factories** and the **Chief Inspector** (in case of death).
2. The notice must be sent **within 24 hours** of the accident.
3. The notice shall be in **Form 18** prescribed under the State Factories Rules, containing particulars like the name of the injured/deceased, nature of injury, date, time, place, and cause of the accident.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is a "dangerous occurrence"? List any four examples as per the Factories Act, 1948.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **"dangerous occurrence"** is an unintended sudden event occurring in a factory which has the potential to cause serious harm to life, limb, or property, even if no personal injury results. These are listed in the **First Schedule** of the Act and are notified under **Section 88A** by the **Occupier to the Chief Inspector within 24 hours** in **Form 18A**.

**Four Examples:**
1. Bursting of a pressure vessel containing steam, compressed air, or gases.
2. Collapse or failure of a crane, derrick, winch, or other lifting appliance.
3. Electrical short-circuit or fire attended with explosion.
4. Collapse or subsidence of any floor, gallery, ceiling, or roof of a building.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*(a)* Explain the statutory procedure for **notice of accidents** under the Factories Act, 1948. Mention the form, time limit, and authority to whom notice is to be sent. **[7 marks]**

*(b)* Discuss the provisions relating to **notice of dangerous occurrences** under Section 88A. How is it different from a notice of accident? **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Notice of Accidents (Section 88) — [7 marks]**

1. **Triggering Events:** An accident in a factory that:
   - Causes **death** of a worker, OR
   - Causes **bodily injury** leading to absence from work for more than **48 hours**.

2. **Person Liable:** The **Manager** of the factory is statutorily responsible for sending the notice.

3. **Time Limit:** The notice must be sent **forthwith (within 24 hours)** of the occurrence of the accident or the death (whichever is applicable).

4. **Authorities to Whom Notice is Sent:**
   - **Inspector of Factories** of the local jurisdiction.
   - **Chief Inspector of Factories** (specifically in case of fatal accidents).

5. **Form:** The notice is submitted in **Form 18** prescribed under the State Factories Rules. It must contain:
   - Name and address of factory
   - Name of Occupier and Manager
   - Name, father's name of injured/deceased
   - Date, time, and place of accident
   - Nature of injury and brief cause
   - First aid/medical treatment given
   - Signature of Manager

6. **Penalty for Default:** Under **Section 92**, failure to give notice is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.

**(b) Notice of Dangerous Occurrences (Section 88A) — [7 marks]**

1. **Definition:** A "dangerous occurrence" is an unintended sudden event having the potential to cause serious harm, even if no actual injury occurs. These are specified in the **First Schedule** of the Act.

2. **Person Liable:** The **Occupier** of the factory (distinct from the Manager who handles Section 88).

3. **Time Limit:** Notice must be sent **within 24 hours** of the dangerous occurrence to the **Chief Inspector of Factories**.

4. **Form:** **Form 18A** is used.

5. **Key Differences from Section 88:**

| Parameter | Section 88 | Section 88A |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Event | Accident causing death/injury | Dangerous occurrence |
| Person Liable | Manager | Occupier |
| Authority | Inspector + Chief Inspector (if death) | Chief Inspector |
| Form | Form 18 | Form 18A |
| Injury Requirement | Injury or death must occur | Injury NOT necessary |

6. **Examples:** Bursting of pressure vessel, crane collapse, electrical short-circuit with fire, roof collapse, gas receiver explosion, hydrogen fire.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the legal provisions for **notice of occupational diseases** under Section 89 of the Factories Act, 1948. What are the obligations of the employer? **[7 marks]**

*(b)* Describe the **inquiry procedure under Section 90** of the Factories Act, 1948. What powers does the inquiry officer possess? **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Notice of Occupational Diseases (Section 89) — [7 marks]**

1. **Triggering Event:** When a worker in a factory contracts any disease listed in the **Second Schedule** (industrial poisoning) or **Third Schedule** (other occupational diseases) of the Factories Act.

2. **Person Liable:** The **Manager** of the factory.

3. **Authorities to be Notified:**
   - **Chief Inspector of Factories** — in **Form 19**
   - **Certifying Surgeon** of the area — in **Form 20**

4. **Time Limit:** The notice must be given **as soon as practicable** (no fixed 24-hour rule).

5. **Key Notifiable Diseases (Memorize):**
   - **Second Schedule:** Lead poisoning, Mercury poisoning, Arsenic poisoning, Phosphorus poisoning, Benzene poisoning, Aniline poisoning.
   - **Third Schedule:** Silicosis, Asbestosis, Bagassosis, Anthrax, Byssinosis, Chrome ulceration, Toxic jaundice, Scrotal cancer, Toxic anaemia.

6. **Obligations of the Employer:**
   - **Removal from job:** The worker must be removed from the specific work that caused the disease (Section 25 of Factories Act).
   - **Alternative employment:** Must be provided on **equal or similar pay**.
   - **Leave with full pay:** Up to **1 year** for treatment and recovery.
   - **Medical examination:** The Certifying Surgeon examines and certifies the disease.
   - **No termination:** The worker cannot be dismissed or discharged during this leave period.

**(b) Inquiry Procedure under Section 90 — [7 marks]**

1. **Authority:** The **State Government** has the power to direct an inquiry into the causes of any **accident** or **occupational disease** in a factory.

2. **When is it Triggered?**
   - On its own motion
   - On the recommendation of the **Chief Inspector of Factories**
   - Following a serious accident or disease outbreak

3. **Inquiry Officer:** The State Government appoints a person (typically a Magistrate, senior Inspector, or retired judicial officer) to conduct the inquiry.

4. **Powers of the Inquiry Officer:**
   - Enter the factory at any reasonable time
   - Inspect premises, machinery, and equipment
   - Examine registers, records, and documents
   - Examine witnesses under **oath**
   - Take samples of materials, substances, or effluents
   - Require production of any document relevant to the inquiry
   - Compel attendance of witnesses

5. **Outcome of Inquiry:**
   - The Inquiry Officer submits a **detailed report** to the State Government.
   - The report contains **findings of fact, causes, and recommendations**.
   - The State Government may then:
     - Order **prosecution** of the offender
     - Direct **remedial measures** to prevent recurrence
     - Order **closure** of the factory in extreme cases
     - Issue **safety guidelines** for the industry

6. **Significance:** The inquiry mechanism ensures **systemic learning** from industrial incidents and forms the basis of evolving safety legislation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "Occupier" with "Manager." Section 88A → **Occupier**; Sections 88 and 89 → **Manager**. This is a **2-mark difference** lost by most students.
> 2. **Do not write** "24 hours" for Section 89 (occupational diseases). The correct phrase is "**as soon as practicable**."
> 3. **Forms 18, 18A, 19, 20** are high-yield recall questions — write them in **bold** in the exam.
> 4. **Section 90 inquiry** is by the **State Government** (not the Inspector). Students often incorrectly attribute the power to the Inspector.
> 5. Cite **Section numbers** explicitly — KTU board examiners allot 1–2 marks simply for proper statutory references.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Notice of Accidents and Inquiries**

- **Section 88:** Accident → death OR injury causing >48 hrs absence → **Manager** → **Inspector + Chief Inspector (if death)** → **Within 24 hours** → **Form 18**.
- **Section 88A:** Dangerous Occurrence (1st Schedule) → **Occupier** → **Chief Inspector** → **Within 24 hours** → **Form 18A**.
- **Section 89:** Occupational Disease (2nd/3rd Schedule) → **Manager** → **Chief Inspector (Form 19) + Certifying Surgeon (Form 20)** → "**As soon as practicable**".
- **Section 90:** **State Government** directs inquiry; Inquiry Officer has powers of inspection, witness examination under oath, and document production.
- **Section 92:** Penalty for failure to notify — imprisonment up to 6 months, fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
- **Key Diseases (2nd Schedule):** Lead, Mercury, Arsenic, Benzene, Phosphorus poisoning.
- **Key Diseases (3rd Schedule):** Silicosis, Asbestosis, Bagassosis, Anthrax, Byssinosis, Scrotal cancer.
- **Key Dangerous Occurrences (1st Schedule):** Pressure vessel burst, crane collapse, gas explosion, electrical short-circuit with fire, roof collapse, hydrogen fire.
- **Employer Obligations (Section 25 + Section 89):** Remove worker from causative job, provide alternative work on equal pay, grant leave with full pay up to 1 year, no dismissal during treatment.
- **Hierarchy:** Manager → Inspector → Chief Inspector → State Government → Inquiry Officer.
- **Memo trick for forms:** "**18 = Accident**, **18A = Dangerous**, **19 = Chief Inspector Disease**, **20 = Surgeon Disease**."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Act - Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT411 - LEGAL ASPECTS OF HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Water Act - Definitions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Water Act — The Legislative Backbone The **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** (commonly called "The Water Act") is the **first comprehensive Indian legislation** enacted by the Parliament of India to address the growing crisis of water pollution in the country. It was enacted under **Article 252 of the Constitution of India** because water is a **State subject** (Entry 17, List II, Seventh Schedule), and the Act came into force only in those States which adopted a resolution consenting to Central legislation on the subject. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Students must be able to recall, explain, and apply the **statutory definitions** laid down under **Section 2** of the Water Act, 1974. These definitions form the basis for all subsequent legal interpretations, liability assessment, and enforcement actions. The Act was later amended by the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Amendment Act, 1988**, which significantly strengthened the enforcement machinery, particularly the powers of the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and the **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Rules for Water" Intuition Imagine a city where every house is allowed to dump **any kind of waste — chemicals, oil, sewage, garbage — directly into the river** flowing through it. The river is a shared resource, and the cumulative dumping would make it toxic, undrinkable, and lifeless. To prevent this chaos, the government creates a **set of rules** that: - **Defines what "waste" means** (so people cannot argue, "this is not waste, it is just coloured water"). - **Identifies who monitors** the river (the Pollution Control Boards). - **Says what is forbidden** and what is permitted (consent mechanism). - **Prescribes penalties** for violations. > [!NOTE] > The Water Act, 1974 functions **exactly like the traffic rules of a city**, but applied to water bodies. Just as traffic rules define "vehicle," "road," "speed limit," etc., the Water Act defines "pollutant," "stream," "sewer," "trade effluent," etc. **Without clear definitions, no prosecution or regulation is possible.** ## 1.3 Objectives of the Water Act, 1974 The Act was enacted with the following explicit objectives, as captured in its long title and Statement of Objects and Reasons: - To **prevent** and **control** water pollution. - To **maintain or restore** the wholesomeness of water in streams and rivers. - To establish **Pollution Control Boards** at the Central and State levels. - To confer upon the Boards the **powers and functions** related to the prevention and control of water pollution. - To prescribe **penalties** for contravention of the provisions of the Act. - To ensure that water quality conforms to prescribed **standards**. ## 1.4 Extent and Application of the Act The Water Act extends to the **whole of India** (after adoption by States under Article 252). It applies to: - All **streams, rivers, wells, sea, or any water body** (natural or artificial). - All **industries, municipalities, and local authorities** discharging effluents. - All **persons** who cause or are likely to cause water pollution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Definition for Exams:** *The Water Act, 1974 is an Act of Parliament to provide for the prevention and control of water pollution and the maintaining or restoring of wholesomeness of water in streams and rivers, and for matters connected therewith.* --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Definitions Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Framework of the Water Act, 1974 The Act is organized into **7 Chapters and 4 Schedules**. Understanding this skeleton is essential for KTU examinations: | **Chapter** | **Sections** | **Subject Matter** | |---|---|---| | **Chapter I** | Sections 1–2 | Preliminary (Short title, extent, commencement, **Definitions**) | | **Chapter II** | Sections 3–12 | The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) — constitution, composition, tenure, powers | | **Chapter III** | Sections 13–25 | **Joint Boards**, **Conferences of State Boards**, and powers of Boards to obtain information | | **Chapter IV** | Sections 26–32 | **Prevention and Control of Water Pollution** — consent mechanism, restriction on new outlets, effluent standards | | **Chapter V** | Sections 33–42 | **Penalty and Procedure** — offences, penalties, cognizance of offences | | **Chapter VI** | Sections 43–50 | **Miscellaneous** — power of State Government to supersede, Power to make rules | | **Chapter VII** | (Omitted by Amendment) | — | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Almost every exam question on the Water Act originates from **Section 2 (Definitions)** and **Chapter II (Boards)** and **Chapter IV (Prevention and Control)**. ## 2.2 KTU Definitions Cheat Sheet — Section 2 of the Water Act, 1974 The following table contains the **most examination-relevant definitions** drawn directly from **Section 2** of the Water Act. These are reproduced using the precise statutory language used by the Indian Parliament. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "define" a term in a 3-mark question, always reproduce the **exact statutory wording** followed by a **brief 1–2 line interpretation in your own words**. This is what examiners award full marks for. | **S.No.** | **Term** | **Statutory Definition (Section 2, Water Act 1974)** | **Plain-English Interpretation** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **"Act"** | Means the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. | The full name of the statute. | | 2 | **"Board"** | Means the Central Board or a State Board constituted under Section 3. | Refers to the CPCB or the relevant SPCB. | | 3 | **"Central Board"** | Means the Central Pollution Control Board constituted under Section 3. | The apex national-level regulatory authority for water (and later air) pollution. | | 4 | **"State Board"** | Means a State Pollution Control Board constituted under Section 4. | The state-level regulatory authority for water pollution. | | 5 | **"Government"** | Means the Central Government. | The Union of India executive. | | 6 | **"State Government"** | Means a State Government specified in the First Schedule. | The executive of the State which has adopted the Act. | | 7 | **"Member"** | Means a member of a Board. | A nominated person on the Board. | | 8 | **"Occupier"** | In relation to any factory or premises, means the person who has control over the affairs of the factory or premises, and includes, in relation to any substance, the person in possession of the substance. | The "controller" of the polluting unit — usually the owner, manager, or person in charge. | | 9 | **"Prescribed"** | Means prescribed by rules made under this Act. | Anything laid down by Government-issued rules. | | 10 | **"Stream"** | Includes — (i) **river**, (ii) **water course** (whether flowing or for the time being dry), (iii) **inland water** whether natural or artificial, (iv) **subterranean waters**, (v) **sea or tidal waters** to such extent as, having regard to the use of such waters, the State Government may specify. | **Extremely broad** — covers virtually every form of water on Earth. | | 11 | **"Sewer"** | Includes a sewer, pipe, channel, or any other conduit, whether open or closed, used or intended to be used for carrying off sewage or trade effluent or other drainage. | A pipe/conduit carrying wastewater. | | 12 | **"Sewage"** | Means sewage, domestic sewage, industrial sewage, or any other mixture of any of them. | The liquid waste carried by sewers — household or industrial. | | 13 | **"Trade Effluent"** | Includes any liquid, gaseous, or solid substance which is discharged from any premises used for carrying on any trade or industry, including any manufacturing process, as a result of such trade or industry, but does not include domestic sewage. | **The defining phrase in the Act** — any waste from industry that is NOT domestic sewage. | | 14 | **"Effluent"** | Includes any sewage, trade effluent, or any other liquid, gaseous, or solid substance, which is likely to cause water pollution. | The umbrella term for any polluting discharge. | | 15 | **"Pollutant"** | Means any toxic, noxious, or polluting substance, including any solid, liquid, gaseous, or radioactive substance discharged into water, which renders water harmful. | The **physical/chemical/biological agent** that pollutes water. | | 16 | **"Water Pollution"** | Means such contamination of water or such change in the physical, chemical, or biological properties of water, or such discharge of any sewage, trade effluent, or any other liquid, gaseous, or solid substance into water, as may, or is likely to, create a nuisance or render such water harmful or injurious to public health or safety, or to domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or other legitimate uses, or to the life and health of animals or plants or of aquatic organisms. | **A long, exhaustive definition** — captures the act, the medium, and the consequences. | | 17 | **"Consent"** | (Implied in Sections 25/26) — written permission granted by the State Board to an industry to discharge effluents. | Operational licence to pollute within limits. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - **"Trade Effluent"** = Industrial liquid/gaseous/solid discharge. Does **NOT** include domestic sewage. > - **"Sewage"** = Includes domestic, industrial, or any mixture. > - **"Effluent"** = The broadest term — includes sewage, trade effluent, or any other polluting substance. > - **"Pollutant"** = The polluting **substance itself**, not the act of discharging. ## 2.3 Why These Definitions Matter — The "Legal Trigger" Concept Every legal prosecution under the Water Act begins with the prosecution establishing: 1. **What was discharged?** → Identify the *effluent / pollutant*. 2. **Where was it discharged?** → Confirm it was into a *stream*. 3. **Who is responsible?** → Identify the *occupier*. 4. **What is the harm?** → Demonstrate *water pollution* as defined. > [!NOTE] > If **any one** of the above four elements cannot be legally defined and proven using Section 2, the prosecution fails. **Definitions are not academic exercises — they are the foundation of enforceability.** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility For an engineer — particularly a **Civil, Environmental, or Chemical Engineer** — these definitions are not just legal jargon. They directly impact: - **Design of Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs)** — must meet the discharge standards (e.g., General Standards for Discharge of Effluents under Schedule VI). - **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** — defining "stream" and "occupier" determines liability scope. - **Consent applications** (Consent to Establish / Consent to Operate under Sections 25/26). - **Site selection for industries** — proximity to "streams" triggers the Act. - **Statutory compliance audits** for ISO 14001 / ESG reporting. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Legal Analysis of Key Definitions Since the Water Act is a **legal text**, the equivalent of "derivation" in this subject is the **exhaustive legal interpretation** of each key definition. Below is the step-by-step analytical breakdown of the **most important definitions** that are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations. ## 3.1 Deep Analysis — "Water Pollution" [Section 2(e)] > **Step 1 — Identify the Triggering Action.** > The definition begins with the phrases: > - *"Such contamination of water"* > - *"Such change in the physical, chemical, or biological properties of water"* > - *"Such discharge of any sewage, trade effluent, or any other liquid, gaseous, or solid substance into water"* > **Step 2 — Identify the Mode of Pollution.** > The Act contemplates **three modes**: > 1. **Direct contamination** of water. > 2. **Change in properties** of water (even without a foreign substance — e.g., heating of water by a thermal power plant, which alters the physical/biological profile). > 3. **Discharge of any substance** into water. > **Step 3 — Identify the Harm Threshold.** > The discharge must be such that it **"may" or "is likely to"** cause one or more of the following: > - **Create a nuisance.** > - **Render water harmful or injurious to public health or safety.** > - Make water unfit for **domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or other legitimate uses**. > - Harm the **life and health of animals, plants, or aquatic organisms**. > **Step 4 — Legal Consequence.** > If all three elements are present, the act qualifies as **"water pollution"** under the Act and the consent / penalty provisions (Sections 24–32) are triggered. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Word in Definition:** *"Likely to"* — the prosecution does **not** have to prove actual harm; it is sufficient to prove that harm is **probable**. This is a **precautionary** drafting style. --- ## 3.2 Deep Analysis — "Stream" [Section 2(j)] > **Step 1 — Identify the Non-Obvious Inclusion.** > The word "stream" is defined to **include** (not exhaustively limited to) the following: > - (i) **River** > - (ii) **Water course** (whether flowing or for the time being **dry**) > - (iii) **Inland water** (natural or artificial — e.g., lakes, tanks, ponds, reservoirs) > - (iv) **Subterranean waters** (underground aquifers, borewells) > - (v) **Sea or tidal waters** — to the extent specified by the State Government > **Step 2 — Significance of the Word "Includes".** > The use of "includes" means the definition is **inclusive, not exhaustive**. Courts can interpret "stream" to cover water bodies not expressly listed, such as **wetlands, estuaries, and man-made canals**. > **Step 3 — The "Dry Watercourse" Innovation.** > The Act's explicit inclusion of a **dry watercourse** is a legislative masterstroke. A polluter cannot escape liability by arguing, *"The river was dry, so I did not pollute it."* The dry bed is still legally a "stream." > **Step 4 — Subterranean Waters.** > Discharge of pollutants into the **ground** (which seeps into underground aquifers) is also covered. This addresses concerns about **deep well injection** of hazardous waste. --- ## 3.3 Deep Analysis — "Trade Effluent" [Section 2(g)] > **Step 1 — Three Possible States.** > Trade effluent is defined to include any substance which is: > - **Liquid**, OR > - **Gaseous**, OR > - **Solid** > **Step 2 — Source Requirement.** > The substance must be discharged from **"any premises used for carrying on any trade or industry, including any manufacturing process"** as a **result of such trade or industry**. > **Step 3 — Negative Exclusion.** > Trade effluent **does NOT include "domestic sewage"**. This is the critical dividing line: > - A **factory canteen's kitchen waste** = domestic sewage (not trade effluent). > - A **factory's chemical process waste** = trade effluent. > **Step 4 — Legal Consequence.** > Discharge of trade effluent without the consent of the State Board is an **offence under Section 24/25** and triggers penalties. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question asks: *"Is domestic sewage a trade effluent?"* The answer is a definitive **NO**. Always remember this exclusion. --- ## 3.4 Deep Analysis — "Occupier" [Section 2(d)] > **Step 1 — Primary Definition.** > The occupier is the person who has **control over the affairs of the factory or premises**. > **Step 2 — Extension to Substances.** > In relation to any substance, the occupier includes the **person in possession of the substance** (e.g., a transporter holding a tank of hazardous chemical). > **Step 3 — Practical Implication.** > The occupier is **not necessarily** the **owner** of the factory. The occupier is the **person in actual control** — could be: > - The Managing Director. > - A Plant Manager. > - A Leaseholder running the factory. > - A Contractor operating a process. > **Step 4 — Significance.** > The occupier is the **person against whom prosecution is launched** under the Water Act. Identifying the correct occupier is the first step in any legal proceeding. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Confusing Pairs of Definitions The following matrix resolves the most **commonly confused** definitional pairs in KTU examinations: | **Confusion** | **Term 1** | **Term 2** | **Key Difference** | |---|---|---|---| | Trade Effluent vs. Sewage | **Trade Effluent** — from industry/trade. Excludes domestic sewage. | **Sewage** — includes domestic, industrial, or any mixture. | Source. Trade effluent must be from a "trade or industry." | | Pollutant vs. Effluent | **Pollutant** — the **substance** itself. | **Effluent** — the **discharge** containing the pollutant. | Substance vs. discharge. | | Stream vs. Sewer | **Stream** — natural/artificial water body receiving water. | **Sewer** — a **conduit** carrying waste. | Water body vs. pipe. | | Occupier vs. Owner | **Occupier** — person in **control** of the premises. | **Owner** — person holding **legal title**. | Control vs. ownership. | | Central Board vs. State Board | **Central Board** — apex body, coordinates, advises. | **State Board** — implements, grants consent, prosecutes. | National vs. State. | --- ## 3.6 Legal Logic Flow — From Definition to Prosecution $$ \text{Discharge} \rightarrow \text{Identify Substance} \rightarrow \text{Is it a Pollutant/Effluent?} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Where was it discharged?} \rightarrow \text{Is it a Stream?} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Who is responsible?} \rightarrow \text{Is there an Occupier?} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Is there consent?} \rightarrow \text{If no} \rightarrow \text{Offence under Section 24/25} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Penalty under Section 41–42} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Every link in this chain is anchored to a **Section 2 definition**. This is why KTU Module 3 emphasizes definitions — they are the **load-bearing pillars** of the entire statute. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Water Act, 1974 The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete structural organization** of the Water Act, mapping the relationship between Definitions (Section 2), Boards, and Enforcement provisions. This is the most important schematic for KTU Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["WATER ACT 1974
THE STATUTE"] --> nodeB["CHAPTER I
PRELIMINARY"] nodeA --> nodeC["CHAPTER II
CENTRAL AND STATE BOARDS"] nodeA --> nodeD["CHAPTER III
JOINT BOARDS AND CONFERENCES"] nodeA --> nodeE["CHAPTER IV
PREVENTION AND CONTROL OF WATER POLLUTION"] nodeA --> nodeF["CHAPTER V
PENALTY AND PROCEDURE"] nodeA --> nodeG["CHAPTER VI
MISCELLANEOUS"] nodeB --> nodeB1["SECTION 1
Short title, extent, commencement"] nodeB --> nodeB2["SECTION 2
DEFINITIONS
Water Pollution, Stream, Trade Effluent, Occupier, Sewer, Sewage, Effluent, Pollutant"] nodeC --> nodeC1["CENTRAL POLLUTION CONTROL BOARD CPCB
SECTION 3"] nodeC --> nodeC2["STATE POLLUTION CONTROL BOARDS SPCBs
SECTION 4"] nodeE --> nodeE1["SECTION 24 PROHIBITION OF USE OF STREAM FOR DISPOSAL OF POLLUTING MATTER"] nodeE --> nodeE2["SECTION 25 RESTRICTIONS ON NEW OUTLETS AND DISCHARGES"] nodeE --> nodeE3["SECTION 26 CONSENT MECHANISM"] nodeF --> nodeF1["SECTION 41 PENALTY FOR CONTRAVENTION OF SECTION 24"] nodeF --> nodeF2["SECTION 42 OFFENCES BY COMPANIES AND GOVERNMENT DEPARTMENTS"] ``` ## 4.2 Definitional Relationship Map — Section 2 Terms The following diagram shows how the **definitions interlock** to form the regulatory chain of the Water Act: ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA1["WATER POLLUTION
Section 2 e"] --> nodeB1["CAUSED BY"] nodeB1 --> nodeA2["DISCHARGE OF"] nodeA2 --> nodeA3["EFFLUENT
Section 2 b"] nodeA2 --> nodeA4["SEWAGE
Section 2 f"] nodeA2 --> nodeA5["TRADE EFFLUENT
Section 2 g"] nodeA3 --> nodeA6["CONTAINS POLLUTANT
Section 2 c"] nodeA5 --> nodeA7["DOMESTIC SEWAGE EXCLUDED"] nodeA1 --> nodeC1["INTO A STREAM
Section 2 j"] nodeC1 --> nodeC2["RIVER"] nodeC1 --> nodeC3["WATER COURSE DRY OR FLOWING"] nodeC1 --> nodeC4["INLAND WATER NATURAL OR ARTIFICIAL"] nodeC1 --> nodeC5["SUBTERRANEAN WATER"] nodeC1 --> nodeC6["SEA OR TIDAL WATER"] nodeA1 --> nodeD1["BY OCCUPIER
Section 2 d"] nodeD1 --> nodeD2["PERSON IN CONTROL OF FACTORY OR PREMISES"] nodeD1 --> nodeD3["PERSON IN POSSESSION OF SUBSTANCE"] ``` ## 4.3 Enforcement Flowchart — From Definition to Penalty ```mermaid flowchart TB start["POLLUTION EVENT OCCURS"] --> q1{"WHAT WAS DISCHARGED?"} q1 -->|"Effluent / Trade Effluent / Sewage"| q2{"WHERE WAS IT DISCHARGED?"} q1 -->|"Not a pollutant"| out1["NOT COVERED BY ACT"] q2 -->|"Into a Stream"| q3{"IS THERE CONSENT UNDER SECTION 25/26?"} q2 -->|"Not into a stream"| out2["OUTSIDE JURISDICTION"] q3 -->|"YES with valid consent"| q4{"ARE STANDARDS COMPLIED WITH?"} q3 -->|"NO consent"| off1["OFFENCE UNDER SECTION 24"] q4 -->|"YES"| out3["NO OFFENCE"] q4 -->|"NO"| off1 off1 --> pen["PENALTY UNDER SECTION 41
IMPRISONMENT UP TO 6 YEARS
OR FINE UP TO RS 10000
OR BOTH"] off1 --> cog["COGNIZANCE OF OFFENCE
SECTION 43 WATER ACT
SECTION 200 CrPC"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the KTU 2024 exam, students are often asked to draw or describe such flow diagrams. Memorizing the **Section 2 definitions** and the **Chapter-wise organization** is the key to scoring full marks in 14-mark questions. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **Define the term "Water Pollution" as given under Section 2(e) of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.** ### Model Answer (3 Marks) Under Section 2(e) of the Water Act, 1974, **"Water Pollution"** means such contamination of water or such change in the physical, chemical, or biological properties of water, or such discharge of any sewage, trade effluent, or any other liquid, gaseous, or solid substance into water, as may, or is likely to, create a nuisance or render such water harmful or injurious to public health or safety, or to domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or other legitimate uses, or to the life and health of animals or plants or of aquatic organisms. In simple terms, water pollution is any act that makes water unfit for its intended uses or harms living organisms. > **Allocation:** 1.5 marks for reproducing the statutory definition; 1.5 marks for the plain-English interpretation. --- > **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` > **Distinguish between "Sewage" and "Trade Effluent" as defined under the Water Act, 1974.** ### Model Answer (3 Marks) | **Basis** | **Sewage [Section 2(f)]** | **Trade Effluent [Section 2(g)]** | |---|---|---| | **Source** | Domestic, industrial, or a mixture. | Discharged from premises used for any **trade, industry, or manufacturing process**. | | **Exclusion** | No specific exclusion. | **Does NOT include domestic sewage.** | | **Scope** | Broader — covers household waste too. | Narrower — only industrial waste. | | **Example** | Water from a residential apartment's kitchen and toilets. | Effluent from a textile dyeing factory. | **Conclusion:** While sewage is an umbrella term covering all wastewater, trade effluent is restricted to industrial discharges and is specifically **excluded** from being classified as sewage. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **(a) Define and explain the term "Occupier" under the Water Act, 1974. Discuss its significance in determining liability for water pollution. (7 Marks)** > > **(b) Critically analyze the definition of "Stream" as given under the Water Act, 1974. Why is this definition considered wider than common understanding? (7 Marks)** ### Model Answer **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Under **Section 2(d)** of the Water Act, 1974, "Occupier" in relation to any factory or premises means the **person who has control over the affairs of the factory or premises**. In relation to any substance, the occupier includes the **person in possession of the substance**. **Key Elements of the Definition:** 1. **Control over affairs** — The occupier need not be the legal owner. The test is **actual control**, not ownership. 2. **Person in possession of substance** — The definition extends to anyone holding a polluting substance, including transporters. 3. **Includes multiple persons** — Where multiple persons have control, all may be considered occupiers. **Examples of Occupier:** - A Managing Director or CEO of a company running a factory. - A Plant Manager given day-to-day control. - A leaseholder operating an industrial unit. - A contractor transporting hazardous waste in a tanker. **Significance in Determining Liability:** 1. **Identifying the Prosecuted Party** — The occupier is the **natural person** against whom prosecution is launched. The definition ensures that the **actual controller** cannot escape liability by hiding behind a corporate veil. 2. **Vicarious Liability** — When a company commits the offence, the occupier (e.g., the director) is **personally liable** under Section 42. 3. **Prevents Evasion** — A polluter cannot avoid prosecution by transferring "control" to a shell entity. 4. **Substance Possession Clause** — Ensures transporters and storage operators are equally responsible. **Conclusion:** The definition of "occupier" is **broad and purposive**, designed to ensure that **whichever person exercises effective control** over the polluting activity or substance is brought within the regulatory net. It is a critical tool in environmental enforcement. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Section 2(j) of the Water Act, 1974 defines **"Stream"** to include: - (i) **River** - (ii) **Water course** (whether flowing or for the time being **dry**) - (iii) **Inland water** (natural or artificial) - (iv) **Subterranean waters** - (v) **Sea or tidal waters** to the extent specified by the State Government **Why the Definition is Wider than Common Understanding:** 1. **"Includes" — not "Means":** The use of the word "includes" makes the definition **inclusive and non-exhaustive**, allowing courts to extend the scope to water bodies not expressly listed, such as wetlands, estuaries, and man-made canals. 2. **Dry Watercourse:** A polluter cannot escape liability by claiming the river was **dry** at the time of discharge. The dry bed remains a "stream" under the Act. 3. **Subterranean Waters:** Discharge of pollutants into the ground (which seeps into aquifers) is covered. This addresses **deep-well injection** of hazardous waste. 4. **Inland Water — Natural or Artificial:** Lakes, ponds, tanks, and **reservoirs** are all covered. 5. **Sea and Tidal Waters:** Coastal discharges fall within the Act's ambit, with the State Government empowered to fix the territorial extent. **Critical Analysis:** The definition reflects the **modern understanding of hydrological connectivity** — that pollution in one part of the water system (e.g., a small stream or an underground aquifer) can affect the entire ecosystem. By adopting an expansive definition, the legislature has ensured **no water body is left outside regulatory protection**. **Conclusion:** The definition of "stream" under the Water Act, 1974 is **deliberately broad and ecologically comprehensive**, far exceeding the ordinary English meaning of the word. It is a **forward-looking definition** designed to address the full spectrum of water pollution risks. --- > **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **(a) Explain the statutory meaning of "Trade Effluent" and "Effluent" under the Water Act, 1974. Highlight the key differences. (7 Marks)** > > **(b) Discuss the importance of definitions in the Water Act, 1974. How do these definitions support the enforcement machinery? (7 Marks)** ### Model Answer (Brief Outline) **Part (a) — 7 Marks** - **Trade Effluent** [Section 2(g)]: Includes any **liquid, gaseous, or solid substance** discharged from any premises used for **trade or industry** as a result of such trade/industry. **Excludes domestic sewage.** - **Effluent** [Section 2(b)]: Includes any **sewage, trade effluent, or any other liquid, gaseous, or solid substance**, which is **likely to cause water pollution**. - **Key Differences:** (i) Scope — Effluent is broader; (ii) Polluting intent — Effluent is qualified by "likely to cause pollution"; (iii) Source — Trade effluent is industry-specific; (iv) Domestic sewage — Excluded from trade effluent but included in effluent. **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Definitions are the **foundation of statutory enforceability**. They: - Provide **legal precision** in identifying what constitutes pollution. - Enable the **CPCB and SPCBs** to frame consent conditions and standards. - Allow **courts** to interpret disputes consistently. - Create **uniform understanding** across States. - **Anchor the consent mechanism** (Sections 25–26) and the **penalty provisions** (Sections 41–42). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Water Act Definitions Questions:** > > 1. **Quoting incomplete definitions.** Always reproduce the **full statutory text**, not a paraphrased version. Examiners award partial marks for an incomplete quotation. > 2. **Confusing "Trade Effluent" with "Sewage".** Remember: **Domestic sewage is NOT trade effluent.** This single distinction is tested every year. > 3. **Forgetting the "dry watercourse" inclusion in "Stream".** Many students write only "river" — this loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Using "Pollutant" and "Effluent" interchangeably.** Pollutant is the **substance**; Effluent is the **discharge**. > 5. **Not mentioning the Section number.** Every definition must be cited with its **Section 2 subsection** (e.g., Section 2(e), 2(g), 2(j)). > 6. **Skipping the plain-English interpretation.** Examiners expect both the **statutory quote** and a **brief explanation** in simple words. > 7. **Mixing up "Central Board" and "State Board" roles.** The Central Board (CPCB) **coordinates and advises**; the State Board (SPCB) **implements and prosecutes**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Water Act, 1974: Definitions** - **Long title of the Act:** *"An Act to provide for the prevention and control of water pollution and the maintaining or restoring of wholesomeness of water in streams and rivers."* - **Enactment year:** 1974; **Enacted under:** Article 252 of the Constitution (because water is a State subject). - **Major Amendment:** Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Amendment Act, **1988**. - **Statutory Definitions** are given in **Section 2** of the Act. > **The 5 Most Important Definitions (Must Memorize):** 1. **Water Pollution [Sec 2(e)]** — Any contamination / change in properties / discharge of any sewage, trade effluent, or other substance into water, which is likely to harm health, safety, or legitimate uses. 2. **Stream [Sec 2(j)]** — Includes river, water course (flowing or dry), inland water (natural or artificial), subterranean waters, sea/tidal waters. 3. **Trade Effluent [Sec 2(g)]** — Any liquid, gaseous, or solid substance discharged from trade/industry. **Excludes domestic sewage.** 4. **Occupier [Sec 2(d)]** — Person who has control over the affairs of the factory or premises, OR person in possession of a substance. 5. **Effluent [Sec 2(b)]** — Includes any sewage, trade effluent, or other substance which is likely to cause water pollution. > **Critical Distinctions to Remember:** - **Sewage ≠ Trade Effluent** (trade effluent excludes domestic sewage). - **Pollutant ≠ Effluent** (pollutant is substance; effluent is the discharge). - **Stream ≠ Sewer** (stream = water body; sewer = pipe). - **Occupier ≠ Owner** (occupier = person in control; owner = person with legal title). - **Central Board ≠ State Board** (CPCB coordinates; SPCB implements). > **One-Line Definitions (Quick Recall):** - **Stream** = River + Dry Riverbed + Inland Water + Underground Water + Sea. - **Trade Effluent** = Industrial Waste (Not Domestic). - **Occupier** = The Person in Control. - **Water Pollution** = Any change in water that makes it unfit. - **Effluent** = Any polluting liquid/gas/solid discharge. > **Exam-Ready Recall (Last-Minute Revision):** > When asked to define a term: (1) State the **Section**; (2) Quote the **statutory definition**; (3) Add a **plain-English interpretation**; (4) Give a **real-world example**; (5) Mention the **legal consequence** (offence/penalty). ---

topics.title: Powers and Functions of Boards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT411 - LEGAL ASPECTS OF HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powers and Functions of Boards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Statutory Boards in Indian HSE Law — Formal Definition

A **Statutory Board**, in the context of Indian Health, Safety and Environment (HSE) legislation, is a *quasi-judicial and quasi-executive body* constituted by an Act of Parliament or State Legislature, endowed with legally defined **powers**, **functions**, **jurisdiction**, and **authority** to regulate, supervise, advise, and enforce compliance with the parent statute. These boards are *creatures of statute* and possess the powers expressly conferred on them under the relevant parent enactment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 of PESFT411, the following Statutory Boards are central to the study:

1. **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** — under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.
2. **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** / State Pollution Control Committees — under the Water Act, 1974 and the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.
3. **Central Board** constituted under the **Air Act, 1981** (functionally CPCB).
4. **Site Appraisal Committees (SAC)** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.
5. **Boards / Committees under the Factories Act, 1948** (e.g., the State Safety Advisory Committee, Safety Committee in factories).
6. **Boards under the Building and Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1996** (e.g., the Building Workers' Welfare Board).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasises the *constitution*, *powers*, *functions*, and *jurisdictional limitations* of boards under the **Factories Act, 1948**, **Water Act, 1974**, **Air Act, 1981**, and **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Board's Role = A Cricket Match Referee"**
>
> Imagine a cricket match. The **Government (Parliament/State Legislature)** is the rulebook author. The **Industry / Polluter / Employer** is the player who *must* follow the rules. The **Statutory Board (CPCB / SPCB / Safety Committee)** is the **on-field umpire + third umpire + match referee** combined.
>
> - It **advises** the player (advisory function) — like telling a bowler, "Be careful about your action."
> - It **monitors** performance (regulatory function) — like reviewing the third-umpire camera.
> - It **orders corrective action** (executive function) — like signalling "OUT" or "NOT OUT."
> - It **imposes penalties** (penal function) — like docking a player's fee.
> - It **settles disputes** (quasi-judicial function) — like the match referee's final say.
>
> Just as a referee cannot rewrite the laws of cricket (only the MCC can), a Board **cannot exceed the powers granted to it by the parent Act** — a doctrine known as the *doctrine of ultra vires*. A board acting beyond its statutory mandate is void and open to judicial review.

---

## 1.3 Statutory Foundations — At a Glance

| S.No. | Board | Parent Act | Year |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) | Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act | **1974** |
| 2 | State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) | Water Act | **1974** |
| 3 | Central Board / SPCBs (Air) | Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act | **1981** |
| 4 | Central Board / State Boards (Water Cess) | Water (Cess) Act | **1977** |
| 5 | Site Appraisal Committees | Environment Protection Act / EIA Notification | **1986 / 1994** |
| 6 | State Safety Advisory Committee | Factories Act | **1948** |
| 7 | Safety Committee (in factories) | Factories Act, Sec. 41G | **1948 (amended 1987)** |
| 8 | Building Workers' Welfare Board | Building and Other Construction Workers' Act | **1996** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory constants for HSE boards (mandatory numerical recall):**
> - CPCB was constituted on **22 September 1974** under Section 3 of the Water Act, 1974.
> - SPCB has the **same powers and functions as CPCB** within the State, *mutatis mutandis* — under **Section 4 of the Water Act, 1974**.
> - **Section 17** of the Water Act, 1974 defines the *functions of CPCB*.
> - **Section 23** of the Air Act, 1981 defines the *functions of the SPCB under the Air Act*.
> - The **SPCB** must have a **minimum of 5 members** and a **maximum of 15 members** (including the Chairman).

---

## 1.4 Legal Nature of Statutory Boards

- **Quasi-Executive** — they execute the provisions of the statute (issuing consents, sampling, monitoring).
- **Quasi-Judicial** — they adjudicate disputes between the regulator and the polluter (e.g., refusal of consent, revocation of consent).
- **Quasi-Legislative** — they frame rules, regulations, and standards (delegated legislation).
- **Body Corporate / Statutory Corporation** — they have a separate legal identity, can hold property, enter contracts, and sue/be sued in their own name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bound by the Act:** A board derives its power **solely from the parent statute**. Any action outside the four corners of the Act is *ultra vires* and void. This was firmly held in *A.K. Kraipak v. Union of India (1970)* — a foundational case on the duties of statutory bodies.

---

## 1.5 Module 3 — Why This Topic Matters

For the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic directly maps to the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO3:** *Apply statutory provisions relating to the functioning of regulatory boards in occupational health, safety, and environmental management.*
- **CO4:** *Examine the quasi-judicial, quasi-executive, and quasi-legislative powers of statutory boards and their role in HSE enforcement.*

These outcomes are tested at the **Apply** and **Analyse** levels of **Bloom's Revised Taxonomy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) — Section 3, Water Act 1974

### 2.1.1 Constitution of CPCB (Section 3)

The CPCB is constituted by the **Central Government** under **Section 3(1)** of the Water Act, 1974. It consists of the following members (as enumerated in the Act and Central Government notifications):

- A **full-time Chairman** — appointed by the Central Government.
- A **full-time Member-Secretary** — appointed by the Central Government.
- **Not more than 5** full-time members.
- **Not more than 19** part-time members, representing:
    * Ministries of the Central Government dealing with industries, health, environment, etc.
    * State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) — at least 2 members.
    * Local authorities — at least 2 members.
    * Non-governmental organisations (NGOs) / persons with special knowledge in environmental matters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Recap (highly tested in KTU ESE):**
> - Chairman: **1 (full-time)**
> - Member-Secretary: **1 (full-time)**
> - Full-time members: **Maximum 5**
> - Part-time members: **Maximum 19**
> - **Total seats: Maximum 26** (as per amendments and notifications).

### 2.1.2 Functions of CPCB (Section 17, Water Act 1974) — *"The Big 16"*

Section 17 of the Water Act, 1974 enumerates the functions of the CPCB. These are subdivided into **advisory**, **regulatory**, **coordinating**, and **technical** functions. Memorising these is **mandatory for KTU ESE**.

The principal functions are:

1. **Advise** the Central Government on matters concerning prevention and control of water pollution.
2. **Coordinate** the activities of the SPCBs and resolve disputes among them.
3. **Lay down standards** for the quality of water in streams, rivers, wells, and other water bodies — in consultation with the State Boards.
4. **Lay down standards** for effluents (the discharge of sewage and trade effluents) on receiving waters.
5. **Advise the Central Government** on the location of any industry that may pollute a stream or well.
6. **Collect and disseminate** information relating to water pollution and the prevention/control thereof.
7. **Prepare, execute, and coordinate** programmes for the prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution.
8. **Mediate** disputes between two or more SPCBs (Section 18).
9. **Establish or recognise** laboratories for analysis of water, sewage, or trade effluents.
10. **Publish** data and statistics concerning water pollution.
11. **Lay down effluent standards** for industries discharging into water bodies.
12. **Plan and execute** a nation-wide programme for water quality monitoring.
13. **Investigate and research** problems of water pollution.
14. **Inspect** any plant, equipment, or process to ensure compliance with the Act.
15. **Issue directions** to the SPCBs as deemed necessary.
16. **Perform such other functions** as may be prescribed.

### 2.1.3 Powers of CPCB

The CPCB possesses the following powers, all derived from the Water Act, 1974:

- **Power to Issue Directions** — Under **Section 18**, CPCB can issue binding directions to any SPCB, which the latter is *mandatorily obliged* to comply with.
- **Power to Take Samples** — Under **Section 21**, the CPCB (or any officer authorised by it) can take samples of any effluent for analysis. Failure to allow sampling is a punishable offence.
- **Power of Entry, Inspection, and Seizure** — Under **Sections 23 to 25**, officers of CPCB (of prescribed rank) can enter any premises at all reasonable times, inspect, take samples, and seize records.
- **Power to Advise on Industry Siting** — Under **Section 17(2)(e)**, CPCB advises the Central Government on the location of industries.
- **Power to Mediate Disputes** — Under **Section 18**, CPCB mediates inter-state water pollution disputes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 18 of the Water Act, 1974 — Dispute Resolution Mechanism**
>
> If there is a dispute between two or more SPCBs, the CPCB shall:
> 1. Hold an inquiry.
> 2. Give a hearing to the concerned SPCBs.
> 3. Pass appropriate orders and directions.
> 4. Such orders are **binding on all parties** to the dispute.
>
> This is a classic example of the **quasi-judicial power** of CPCB.

### 2.1.4 The Doctrine of "Binding Directions" — Section 18-A

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** Under **Section 18-A (inserted by Amendment Act, 1988)**, where the Central Government is of the opinion that any SPCB has failed to comply with the directions issued under Section 18, it can — *in the public interest* — **issue directions to the State Government** to ensure compliance. The State Government is then *legally bound* to give effect to such directions.

This is one of the most-cited KTU exam questions in Module 3.

---

## 2.2 State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) — Section 4, Water Act 1974

### 2.2.1 Constitution of SPCB

Constituted by the **State Government** under **Section 4(1)**. The Chairman is **full-time**, nominated by the State Government. The composition is similar to CPCB but with State-level representation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Composition rule (Sec. 4):**
> - Minimum: **5 members**
> - Maximum: **15 members**
> - Chairman: 1 (full-time)

### 2.2.2 Functions of SPCB (Section 17, *mutatis mutandis*)

The SPCB has the **same functions as CPCB**, applied within the territorial jurisdiction of the State. Additionally, the SPCB has exclusive authority to:

- **Grant or refuse consent** to establish or operate any industry, trade, process, or operation that may discharge effluents — under **Section 25 of the Water Act, 1974**.
- **Lay down effluent standards** at the State level, subject to the standards laid down by the CPCB.
- **Advise the State Government** on matters concerning water pollution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consent to Establish & Consent to Operate** — A non-negotiable KTU question:
>
> 1. **Consent to Establish (CTE)** — Issued *before* the industry is set up. Ensures the proposed industry will incorporate pollution control measures at the design stage. Valid for a stipulated period.
> 2. **Consent to Operate (CTO)** — Issued *after* the industry is established and pollution control systems are installed. Valid for **5 years** (renewable).
>
> Without these consents, the industry cannot legally operate in India.

### 2.2.3 Powers of SPCB (Section 23–25, Water Act)

- **Power to grant, refuse, or revoke consent** — Section 25.
- **Power to enter and inspect** — Section 23.
- **Power to take samples** — Section 21.
- **Power to issue directions** — Section 33-A.
- **Penalty power** — Recommend prosecution for offences under Section 24.

---

## 2.3 The Central Board / SPCBs under the Air Act, 1981

### 2.3.1 Background

The **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** was enacted to provide for the prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution. It was modeled closely on the Water Act, 1974.

> [!NOTE]
> **Co-terminus structure:** The CPCB under the Water Act is **also** designated as the **Central Board** under the Air Act. Similarly, the SPCB under the Water Act is the **State Board** under the Air Act for the same State — unless the State Government notifies otherwise.

### 2.3.2 Functions of the State Board under the Air Act (Section 17)

The State Board under the Air Act performs, *mutatis mutandis*, all the functions of the CPCB, **plus**:

1. **Plan and execute** a comprehensive programme for air pollution control.
2. **Advise the State Government** on air pollution matters.
3. **Lay down standards** for the quality of air (ambient air quality standards).
4. **Lay down emission standards** for industrial plants, automobiles, and other sources.
5. **Inspect air pollution control equipment** in industrial premises.

### 2.3.3 Powers under the Air Act (Sections 22–26)

- **Power to consent** for establishment / operation of an industry (Section 21).
- **Power to declare air pollution control areas** (Section 19).
- **Power to enter, inspect, and take samples** (Section 22).
- **Power to issue directions** (Section 31-A).

---

## 2.4 Boards under the Factories Act, 1948

### 2.4.1 The State Safety Advisory Committee (Section 10)

Under **Section 10(1)** of the Factories Act, 1948, the State Government may constitute a **Chief Inspector of Factories and a State Advisory Committee** to advise the State Government on matters relating to:
- Safety of workers in factories.
- Health and welfare of workers.
- Effective implementation of the Act.

> [!NOTE]
> **Composition:** The Committee must include:
> - The Chief Inspector of Factories (ex-officio member).
> - A representative of the State Government.
> - Representatives of employers.
> - Representatives of workers.
> - A person with special knowledge of factory matters.
>
> The Committee is **purely advisory** — *not* quasi-judicial.

### 2.4.2 The Safety Committee (Section 41-G)

Under **Section 41-G** (inserted by the Amendment Act of 1987), every factory employing **250 or more workers** (or such number as the State Government prescribes) must constitute a **Safety Committee**.

**Functions of the Safety Committee:**
- Promote cooperation between workers and management for safety.
- Identify hazards and suggest remedial measures.
- Review safety performance periodically.
- Discuss safety-related incidents, near-misses, and accidents.
- Ensure compliance with statutory safety provisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Recap (Factories Act):**
> - **250 workers** → Safety Committee mandatory.
> - **1,000 workers or hazardous process** → Safety Officer mandatory.
> - **Safety Committee** is *not* a statutory Board per se, but functions as a *de facto* safety governance body.

### 2.4.3 Powers of Safety Committee

- **Recommending** safety measures to the management.
- **Reviewing** accident reports and safety audit findings.
- **Consulting** with the Chief Inspector on safety matters.
- **Mandatory worker representation** — at least 50% of the Committee's members must represent workers.

---

## 2.5 The Building Workers' Welfare Board — Section 16, BOCW Act 1996

Under **Section 16** of the Building and Other Construction Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1996, the State Government constitutes a **State Building and Other Construction Workers' Welfare Board** for the welfare of building workers.

### Functions of the Welfare Board:
1. **Administer** the Building and Other Construction Workers' Welfare Fund.
2. **Provide financial assistance** for:
    * Children's education.
    * Maternity benefits.
    * Pension.
    * Medical treatment.
    * Housing loans.
3. **Register** building workers.
4. **Issue** identity cards.
5. **Sanction** welfare schemes from time to time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Funding Source:** 1% to 2% of the cost of construction as notified by the State Government, levied on the employer.

---

## 2.6 The Site Appraisal Committee (SAC) — EIA Notification 2006

Under the **Environment Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006** (issued under the Environment Protection Act, 1986), the Central Government constitutes a **Sector-specific Site Appraisal Committee (SAC)** for appraising projects.

### Functions of the SAC:
- **Appraise** Category A projects based on their EIA reports.
- **Recommend** to the Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC) for clearance.
- **Site-specific assessment** for projects.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — *Board-wise at a Glance*

> [!NOTE]
> **No mathematical formulas apply in this law module. The "Formula Sheet" below is a high-yield comparative table for board composition and powers.**

### Comparative Matrix of Key Boards

| Board | Constitution Sec. | Min-Max Members | Tenure / Term | Key Power | Key Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CPCB | Water Act Sec. 3 | Max 26 (1 Ch, 1 MS, 5 FT, 19 PT) | As per rules | Sec. 18 — binding directions to SPCBs | Sec. 17 — advisory + standard-setting |
| SPCB | Water Act Sec. 4 | 5 to 15 (incl. Chairman) | As per State rules | Sec. 25 — grant/refuse consent | Sec. 17 — State-level enforcement |
| Central Board (Air) | Air Act Sec. 5 | Same as CPCB | Same | Sec. 31A — directions | Sec. 17 — emission standards |
| State Board (Air) | Air Act Sec. 5 | Same as SPCB | Same | Sec. 21 — consent to operate | Sec. 17 — air quality monitoring |
| State Safety Advisory Committee | Factories Act Sec. 10 | As per State rules | As per State rules | Advisory only | Advise State Govt on safety |
| Safety Committee | Factories Act Sec. 41G | As per factory rules | Standing committee | Recommending safety measures | Promote safety culture |
| Building Workers' Welfare Board | BOCW Act Sec. 16 | As per State rules | As per State rules | Disburse welfare benefits | Administer Welfare Fund |
| Site Appraisal Committee | EIA Notification 2006 | Sector-specific | Standing | Appraise projects | Recommend clearance |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of HSE Boards in Engineering Practice

- **Industrial Project Approval:** No major industrial project can be set up in India without the **Consent to Establish** from the SPCB and **Environmental Clearance (EC)** from the MoEFCC (assisted by SAC/EAC).
- **Ongoing Operations:** Industries must obtain **Consent to Operate** from the SPCB, valid for **5 years** and renewable.
- **Legal Compliance Audit:** HSE officers must periodically audit compliance with the directions issued by CPCB/SPCB.
- **Penalties for Non-Compliance:** Boards can recommend prosecution, closure, or revocation of consent.
- **Welfare Implementation:** Building Workers' Welfare Boards disburse crores of rupees in welfare benefits annually to construction workers.
- **Data for Engineering Decisions:** CPCB's **National Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)** publishes continuous monitoring data, used by civil and environmental engineers for site selection and design.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> **For this Module 3 legal topic, the "derivations" are not mathematical but are *legal-interpretive analyses* — a step-by-step analytical breakdown of board functions and powers. This is the format expected by KTU examiners in the 14-mark long-answer questions.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Deconstruction of "Powers" of a Statutory Board

The **powers** of any statutory board under HSE law are, on analysis, a composite of **four functional categories**. The KTU 2024 Scheme examiners often test whether the student can correctly *classify* a given power. The analytical matrix is as follows:

### Step 1 — Identify the Source
Every power must be traced back to a **specific section** of the parent Act. A board that cites *no statutory provision* for its action acts *ultra vires*.

### Step 2 — Classify the Power

A power is classified into one of four categories:

$$\boxed{\text{Power} = \text{Advisory} \cup \text{Executive} \cup \text{Quasi-Judicial} \cup \text{Quasi-Legislative}}$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Scope

The scope of the power is **territorial** (e.g., CPCB — all-India; SPCB — State), **subject-matter** (e.g., water pollution under the Water Act), and **temporal** (e.g., during the operational life of the board).

### Step 4 — Check for Safeguards

Every statutory power is bounded by:
- **Natural justice** (audi alteram partem — hear the other side).
- **Reasoned orders** — every quasi-judicial order must be speaking and reasoned.
- **Judicial review** — Articles 226 and 227 (High Court) and Article 32/136 (Supreme Court).

### Step 5 — Verify Constitutional Compliance

The board's action must comply with **Article 14** (equality), **Article 19(1)(g)** (freedom to carry on business), and **Article 21** (right to life and livelihood).

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Exercise — *Classification of Powers of CPCB*

> **KTU-Style Analytical Question:**
> *"Classify the following powers of the CPCB into Advisory, Executive, Quasi-Judicial, and Quasi-Legislative categories, citing the relevant statutory provision for each."*

### Step-by-Step Solution (Model Answer Framework):

| Power (Given) | Statutory Section | Classification | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Advise the Central Government on industry siting | Sec. 17(2)(e), Water Act | **Advisory** | The act of advising is non-binding and consultative. |
| Lay down standards for effluents | Sec. 17(2)(c) & (d), Water Act | **Quasi-Legislative** | Framing standards is delegated legislation. |
| Mediate disputes between SPCBs | Sec. 18, Water Act | **Quasi-Judicial** | Inquiry + hearing + binding decision = adjudicatory. |
| Take samples of effluents from an industry | Sec. 21, Water Act | **Executive** | Investigation and inspection are executive functions. |
| Issue directions to SPCBs to comply with standards | Sec. 18, Water Act | **Executive** (binding in effect) | Direction is administrative in form but quasi-legislative in substance. |
| Inspect industrial premises | Sec. 23, Water Act | **Executive** | Pure inspection = executive function. |
| Grant or refuse consent to a State Board for specific discharges | Sec. 25, Water Act (by analogy, for SPCBs) | **Quasi-Judicial** | Affects rights of the party; requires hearing. |
| Establish or recognise labs | Sec. 17(2)(i), Water Act | **Executive** | Administrative act. |
| Publish data and statistics | Sec. 17(2)(j), Water Act | **Advisory + Informational** | Promotes public awareness. |
| Prosecute offenders | Sec. 24, Water Act | **Executive (in aid of quasi-judicial process)** | The Board files a complaint, but the Court adjudicates. |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — *Hierarchy of Powers from Constitution to Board*

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Constitution of India} &\xrightarrow{\text{Art. 253}} \text{International Treaty (Stockholm 1972)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Art. 48A + 51A(g)}} \text{Directive Principle (State Duty)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Legislative Competence, Sch. VII}} \text{Entry 17, List II (Water)} \\
&\text{and Entry 24, List I (Environment)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Constitutional Mandate}} \text{Parliamentary Enactment} \\
&\text{(Water Act 1974 / Air Act 1981 / EP Act 1986)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Section 3 / 4 / 5}} \text{Constitution of CPCB / SPCB / Central Board} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Section 17 / 18 / 21 / 25}} \text{Statutory Powers and Functions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic of each transition:**

- **Step 1 → Art. 253:** The Stockholm Conference (1972) on the Human Environment motivated India to pass the Water Act 1974. Article 253 enables Parliament to legislate on treaty implementation.
- **Art. 48A + 51A(g):** The 42nd Amendment (1976) inserted environmental duties — protection of the environment and wildlife (State duty under 48A; fundamental duty of citizens under 51A(g)).
- **Sch. VII, Entry 17, List II:** "Water" — including water supply, irrigation, canals, drainage, embankments, water storage — is a State subject, allowing the State to legislate.
- **Sch. VII, Entry 24, List I:** "Ecology and Environment" — is a Concurrent subject only if added; currently a Central subject, allowing Union to legislate.
- **Constitutional Mandate → Parliamentary Enactment:** Parliament enacts the Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, and EP Act 1986.
- **Section 3 / 4 / 5:** The Acts *constitute* the boards and define their membership.
- **Section 17 / 18 / 21 / 25:** The Acts *confer* specific functions, powers, and duties on the boards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This entire chain of derivation is a high-yield KTU 14-mark question, often framed as:**
> *"Trace the constitutional and statutory basis for the constitution of the Central Pollution Control Board. Discuss its functions and powers under the Water Act, 1974."* (10 marks on derivation, 4 marks on functions).

---

## 3.4 The "Quasi-Judicial" Power — Detailed Analysis

A board acts *quasi-judicially* when it:
1. **Determines questions of right or obligation** affecting a specific person or industry.
2. **Is bound by the principles of natural justice** (hear the other side, decide without bias).
3. **Issues a reasoned order** (speaking order).

**Illustrative case study:** The SPCB's refusal to grant *Consent to Operate* to a factory.

- The factory has a **legal right** to seek consent.
- The SPCB's refusal **affects that right**.
- The SPCB must give a **hearing** to the factory (audi alteram partem).
- The SPCB must give a **reasoned order** (speaking order) for refusal.
- The factory can **appeal** to the appellate authority (Central Government or designated authority).

> [!NOTE]
> **Landmark case — *Municipal Corporation of Delhi v. Girdhari Lal (1981)*:** A quasi-judicial authority must record reasons for its decision. A non-speaking order is bad in law and liable to be set aside in judicial review.

---

## 3.5 Power-Vs-Function — A Subtle Distinction

KTU examiners often test the **distinction between "powers" and "functions"**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} &= \text{A legal *capacity* to do something (e.g., issue directions, take samples, prosecute).} \\
\text{Function} &= \text{A legal *duty* to be performed (e.g., advise Government, lay down standards).}
\end{aligned}$$

A function is the **task** assigned; a power is the **instrument** to perform the task. Without the power, the function is unenforceable. Without the function, the power is purposeless.

**Example from CPCB:**

- **Function:** Lay down effluent standards (Sec. 17(2)(c)) — *what CPCB must do.*
- **Power:** Issue binding directions to SPCBs to enforce those standards (Sec. 18) — *the means to compel compliance.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This distinction is a guaranteed 4-mark question in KTU ESE Part B (sub-part).** Always structure your answer as:
> 1. *Function* (the *what*) → cite Section 17.
> 2. *Power* (the *how*) → cite Section 18.
> 3. *Operational reality* (the *in practice*) → cite case law or actual enforcement data.
