# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Pattern (ESE):**
> - Part A: 3 questions × 3 marks = 9 marks.
> - Part B: 2 questions × 14 marks (with internal choice, attempted 1) = 14 marks.
> - **Total: 23 marks** for this module in a typical KTU ESE.
> - Each Part B question has sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 3]*

> **"State the composition of the Central Pollution Control Board as per the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As per **Section 3 of the Water Act, 1974**, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) consists of:

- A **full-time Chairman** (1 mark)
- A **full-time Member-Secretary** (0.5 mark)
- **Not more than 5** full-time members (0.5 mark)
- **Not more than 19** part-time members representing Central Government ministries, State Pollution Control Boards, local authorities, and NGOs (1 mark)

Thus, the CPCB has a **maximum of 26 members** in total, appointed by the Central Government.

---

### Question 2: *[KTU University Exam — December 2022, Module 3]*

> **"Distinguish between 'powers' and 'functions' of a statutory board, citing examples from the Water Act, 1974."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Functions** (1.5 marks):
- Functions are the *duties* or *tasks* assigned to the board by statute.
- Example: Under **Section 17(2)(e)** of the Water Act, 1974, the CPCB must *advise* the Central Government on the location of industries that may pollate streams or wells.

**Powers** (1.5 marks):
- Powers are the *legal capacity or instruments* to perform the functions.
- Example: Under **Section 18** of the Water Act, 1974, the CPCB has the *power* to issue *binding directions* to SPCBs.

In short, *function* is the *what* (the task), and *power* is the *how* (the means to enforce).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]*

> **(a)** Discuss the constitution and functions of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Examine the powers of the State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) under the Water Act, 1974 and the Air Act, 1981. How are these powers distinct from those of the CPCB? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part A(a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is the apex statutory body constituted under **Section 3(1)** of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974, by the Central Government. It is headquartered in **Delhi**. The CPCB operates as a **body corporate** with perpetual succession, capable of holding property and entering into contracts.

**Constitution (2 marks):**
Under Section 3, the CPCB comprises:
- A **full-time Chairman** appointed by the Central Government.
- A **full-time Member-Secretary**.
- **Not more than 5** full-time members.
- **Not more than 19** part-time members, representing:
  - Central Government ministries (industries, health, environment, etc.).
  - State Pollution Control Boards (at least 2).
  - Local authorities (at least 2).
  - Non-governmental organisations / persons with special knowledge of environmental matters.

The total membership is capped at **26 members** (as amended by the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Amendment Act, 1988).

**Functions under Section 17 (4 marks):**
Section 17 enumerates **16 principal functions** of the CPCB, grouped into four categories:

1. **Advisory functions:** Advise the Central Government on water pollution, location of industries, and related matters.
2. **Regulatory functions:** Coordinate the activities of SPCBs, resolve disputes, and issue binding directions under Section 18.
3. **Standard-setting (Quasi-Legislative) functions:** Lay down standards for water quality in streams, rivers, wells; and standards for effluents discharged into water bodies.
4. **Promotional and Research functions:** Collect and disseminate information, plan and execute national programmes for pollution control, establish laboratories, and publish statistics.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The CPCB is a **quasi-executive, quasi-legislative, and quasi-judicial** body that functions as the **national coordinator** of water and air pollution control activities in India.

---

#### Part A(b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) are constituted by the **State Government** under **Section 4(1)** of the Water Act, 1974, and operate within the territorial jurisdiction of the State. Under the Air Act, 1981, the same SPCB is designated as the **State Board** for air pollution control, ensuring administrative convergence.

**Powers of SPCB (4 marks):**

The SPCB possesses the following powers:

1. **Consent Power (Section 25, Water Act; Section 21, Air Act):** The most significant power — to grant, refuse, or revoke *Consent to Establish* and *Consent to Operate* to industrial units.
2. **Power to Inspect (Section 23, Water Act; Section 22, Air Act):** Enter any premises at reasonable times, inspect plant, equipment, and records.
3. **Power to Sample (Section 21, Water Act; Section 26, Air Act):** Take samples of effluents, emissions, and ambient air for analysis.
4. **Power to Declare Pollution Control Areas (Section 19, Air Act):** Declare specific areas as *air pollution control areas* with stricter standards.
5. **Power to Issue Directions (Section 33A, Water Act; Section 31A, Air Act):** Issue binding directions to industries for compliance.
6. **Power to Prosecute (Section 24, Water Act):** File complaints before the Court for offences under the Act.

**Distinct from CPCB (2 marks):**
- **CPCB** is the *national coordinator* and *standard-setter*; **SPCB** is the *local enforcer* and *consent-grantor*.
- CPCB has the **power to issue binding directions to SPCBs**; SPCBs do not have the reverse power over CPCB.
- CPCB mediates **inter-state** disputes (Section 18); SPCBs operate within the State.
- The SPCB has the **exclusive power to grant consent** under the Water Act and the Air Act — a power not exercisable by the CPCB directly (though CPCB may issue directions to the SPCB to do so).

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The SPCB is the *operational arm* of India's environmental governance, while the CPCB is the *strategic and coordinative* arm. Together, they form a **federal regulatory architecture** for pollution control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*: *[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 3]*

> **(a)** Explain the constitution, functions, and powers of the Safety Committee as mandated by the Factories Act, 1948. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the role of the Site Appraisal Committee (SAC) under the Environment Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006. How does the SAC contribute to environmental governance? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part B(a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Safety Committee** is a statutory body mandated by **Section 41-G** of the Factories Act, 1948 (inserted by the Factories (Amendment) Act, 1987). It is to be constituted in every factory employing **250 or more workers**, or such number as the State Government may prescribe.

**Constitution (1.5 marks):**
The Safety Committee is constituted by the **Occupier** of the factory and consists of an equal number of representatives of:
- The employer (management).
- The workers (recognised trade union representatives).
- **Mandatory inclusion of the Safety Officer** and **Factory Medical Officer**.

The total number of members and the **chairperson** are determined as per the State Factory Rules.

**Functions (3 marks):**
Under the Factories Act and the State Rules, the Safety Committee performs the following functions:
1. **Promote cooperation** between workers and management on safety matters.
2. **Identify hazards** in the workplace and suggest remedial measures.
3. **Review** the safety performance of the factory periodically.
4. **Discuss** accidents, near-misses, and dangerous occurrences.
5. **Ensure compliance** with statutory safety provisions of the Factories Act and the State Rules.
6. **Promote** safety training, drills, and awareness among workers.
7. **Liaise** with the Chief Inspector of Factories on safety matters.

**Powers (1.5 marks):**
The Safety Committee is **advisory and recommendatory in nature**:
- It can *recommend* safety measures to the Occupier.
- It can *review* accident reports and safety audit findings.
- It can *request* the Occupier to take corrective actions.
- It does **not** have executive, quasi-judicial, or quasi-legislative powers.
- However, its recommendations are **mandatorily considered** by the Occupier, and the Occupier must report the action taken to the Chief Inspector.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The Safety Committee is a vital mechanism for **participative safety governance** in factories, embedding worker representation in the safety decision-making process.

---

#### Part B(b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Site Appraisal Committee (SAC)** is constituted by the **Central Government** under the **Environment Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification, 2006**, issued in exercise of powers conferred by the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. The SAC is a **sector-specific** body that appraises projects under **Category A** (those requiring Central Government clearance).

**Constitution (1.5 marks):**
The SAC is constituted for specific sectors (e.g., thermal power, mining, infrastructure, river valley projects, etc.) and consists of experts in:
- Environmental engineering.
- Ecology and biodiversity.
- Project engineering.
- Public health.
- Social impact assessment.

The members are drawn from government departments, research institutions, and independent experts.

**Functions (3 marks):**
1. **Appraise** Category A projects on the basis of their **EIA reports** and **Environmental Management Plans (EMPs)**.
2. **Verify** the data and information submitted by the project proponent.
3. **Conduct site visits** where necessary.
4. **Examine** the compliance history of the project proponent.
5. **Recommend** to the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC)** whether or not to grant environmental clearance.
6. **Suggest** conditions and safeguards to be imposed on the project.
7. **Examine** the post-project monitoring plans.

**Role in Environmental Governance (1.5 marks):**
- The SAC acts as a **technical filter** between the project proponent and the final decision-maker (EAC → MoEFCC).
- It brings **domain expertise** to the appraisal process, ensuring that each sector's specific environmental challenges are evaluated.
- It promotes **scientific rigour** in decision-making, reducing the scope for arbitrary clearances.
- It enhances **transparency** by publishing appraisal reports.
- It enables **decentralisation** of technical assessment, with the EAC providing the final policy-level review.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The SAC is a **crucial pillar of India's environmental clearance regime**, ensuring that industrial development is balanced against ecological sustainability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 Answers (KTU 2024 ESE)**
>
> 1. **Omitting Section Numbers:** Examiners deduct up to 1 mark per sub-part if you do not cite the *exact section number* of the Act. Always write "Section 17, Water Act 1974" — never just "the Act says…"
>
> 2. **Confusing CPCB and SPCB Powers:** A frequent error is attributing the *power to grant consent* to the CPCB. **SPCB grants consent**; CPCB *directs* the SPCB. Lose 1–2 marks if confused.
>
> 3. **Mislabelling "Quasi-Judicial":** Many students tag *every* board function as "quasi-judicial." Reserve this label for functions that adjudicate disputes between parties. Routine inspections and sample collection are *executive*, not quasi-judicial.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Numerical Trivia:** "How many members in CPCB?" "What's the worker threshold for Safety Committee?" "Validity of Consent to Operate?" These are guaranteed 1-mark each. Memorise them.
>
> 5. **Not Distinguishing "Powers" from "Functions":** A 4-mark question explicitly asks the distinction. If you club them together, marks are deducted for lack of analytical clarity.
>
> 6. **No Case Law:** A 14-mark answer that omits any case law reference (e.g., *A.K. Kraipak*, *Maneka Gandhi*, *Municipal Corporation of Delhi v. Girdhari Lal*) loses 1–2 marks. Examiners expect at least one *relevant* case reference.
>
> 7. **Weak Conclusion:** Always end with a **one-line synthesis** linking the board's powers to *effective HSE governance*. This signals a high-order thinking skill (Bloom's *Evaluate* level) and earns the last 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Powers and Functions of Boards" (Module 3)**

- ✅ **CPCB** — constituted under **Section 3, Water Act 1974**; functions under **Section 17**; powers under **Section 18, 21, 23–25**.
- ✅ **SPCB** — constituted under **Section 4, Water Act 1974**; consent power under **Section 25**; composition: **5–15 members**.
- ✅ **Central Board (Air)** — same as CPCB for Air Act 1981; functions under **Section 17, Air Act**.
- ✅ **State Board (Air)** — same as SPCB; consent power under **Section 21, Air Act**.
- ✅ **Section 18, Water Act** — *binding directions* from CPCB to SPCBs.
- ✅ **Section 25, Water Act** — *Consent to Establish* and *Consent to Operate*.
- ✅ **Consent to Operate validity — 5 years** (renewable).
- ✅ **Factories Act, Sec. 41-G** — Safety Committee for **250+ worker** factories.
- ✅ **Factories Act, Sec. 10** — State Safety Advisory Committee (advisory only).
- ✅ **BOCW Act, Sec. 16** — Building Workers' Welfare Board for welfare schemes.
- ✅ **EIA Notification 2006** — Site Appraisal Committee (SAC) for Category A projects.
- ✅ **CPCB** is a **body corporate** with quasi-executive, quasi-legislative, and quasi-judicial functions.
- ✅ **Powers of a board are derived from the parent Act** — actions *ultra vires* the Act are void (*A.K. Kraipak v. Union of India*).
- ✅ **Quasi-judicial orders must be speaking and reasoned** (*Municipal Corporation of Delhi v. Girdhari Lal*).
- ✅ **Section 18-A, Water Act** — Central Government can issue directions to State Government to compel SPCB compliance.
- ✅ **Doctrine of natural justice (audi alteram partem)** binds every quasi-judicial action of a board.
- ✅ **Powers ≠ Functions** — Powers are the *means*; functions are the *tasks*.
- ✅ **Federal architecture** — CPCB (national, coordinative) + SPCB (State, enforcement) = federal regulatory system.
- ✅ **Numerical Trivia Recap:**
  - CPCB max members: **26**
  - SPCB min/max members: **5 / 15**
  - Safety Committee threshold: **250 workers**
  - Safety Officer threshold: **1,000 workers** or hazardous process
  - Consent to Operate validity: **5 years**
  - CPCB constituted on: **22 September 1974**
  - Air Act enacted: **1981**
  - Environment Protection Act: **1986**
  - BOCW Act: **1996**
  - EIA Notification: **2006** (with subsequent amendments)

---

Cross-reference with the Factories Act 1948, Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Environment Protection Act 1986, and BOCW Act 1996 for full statutory context.*

topics.title: Provisions Regarding Prevention and Control of Water Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT411 - LEGAL ASPECTS OF HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Provisions Regarding Prevention and Control of Water Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Statutory Definition

The **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** (hereinafter referred to as the **Water Act, 1974**) is the primary Indian legislation enacted by the Parliament of India under **Article 252(1)** of the Constitution, with the primary objective of **preventing and controlling water pollution** and maintaining or restoring the *wholesomeness of water* in the country. It was assented to by the President on **23rd March 1974** and came into force on **23rd March 1974**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Title of the Act:** *"An Act to provide for the prevention and control of water pollution and the maintaining or restoring of wholesomeness of water for the establishment, with a view to carrying out the purposes aforesaid, of Boards for the prevention and control of water pollution, and for matters connected therewith."*

### 1.2 Key Statutory Definitions (Section 2 of the Water Act, 1974)

The Act defines several crucial terms that form the legal backbone of water pollution jurisprudence:

- **"Water Pollution"** means such contamination of water or such change in its physical, chemical or biological properties, or such discharge of any sewage, or trade effluent, or of any other liquid, gaseous or solid substance into water, as may, or is likely to, create a nuisance or render such water harmful or injurious to public health or safety, or to domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural or other legitimate uses, or to the life and health of animals or plants or of aquatic organisms.

- **"Pollutant"** means any toxic or noxious substance, or any other substance, the presence of which in water renders, or is likely to render, the water impurified, noxious or harmful.

- **"Sewage Effluent"** means affluent from a sewer, or from a sewage treatment plant or work, or from any other source of pollution.

- **"Trade Effluent"** means any liquid, gaseous or solid substance which is discharged from any premises used for carrying on any **industrial or trade operation**, and includes any matter, whether or not it is in solution or suspension.

- **"Stream"** includes **river, water course, inland water, well, sea, tidal water, estuary, canal, lake, pond, spring, reservoir, or any other body of water** (natural or artificial).

- **"Occupier"** in relation to any factory or premises, means the person who has the **control over the affairs** of the factory or premises.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Water Act as a "Doctor for India's Waterways"**
>
> Imagine a country's water bodies (rivers, lakes, ponds, sea) as the **circulatory system of a giant living organism**. When factories, cities, or farms release harmful chemicals into this system, it is akin to **injecting poison into the bloodstream** — the entire body (ecosystem, humans, animals, plants) suffers.
>
> The Water Act, 1974, is like a **national health authority** for this water-body organism. It sets up:
> - A **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** → the "Cardiologist" working at the national level
> - **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** → the "Local General Physicians" in every state
> - **Joint Boards** → "Specialist Teams" for interstate water disputes
>
> The Act grants these "doctors" the powers to: **inspect** (right to enter), **diagnose** (right to take samples), **prescribe treatment** (consent to establish and operate), and **impose penalties** (fines/imprisonment) on the "polluters" who act as "toxins" harming the system.

### 1.4 Constitutional Backing and Extent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Article 252(1) Backbone:** The Act was initially adopted because resolutions were passed by the Legislatures of multiple states (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Karnataka, etc.) consenting to Parliament's intervention. It extends to the **whole of India** and applies to all states that adopted the resolution. Currently, **all 28 states and 8 Union Territories** are covered under the Act.

### 1.5 Salient Features of the Act

- **Preventive and Curative** in nature — focuses on prevention before pollution occurs
- **Establishes a dual-tier regulatory structure** — Central and State Boards
- **Provides for penalties** for contravention (fine up to **₹1 lakh** + additional fine up to **₹5,000 per day** for continuing offence)
- **Empowers state governments** to restrict the establishment of industries in certain areas
- **Recognizes citizen's right** — any person may file a complaint regarding water pollution
- **Establishes water quality standards** for various uses (drinking, bathing, industrial cooling, etc.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Organizational Structure of Regulatory Authorities

The Act establishes a **three-tier regulatory framework**:

#### A. Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) — Section 3
- Constituted by the **Central Government** under Section 3(1)
- Headquarters: **Delhi**
- Chairperson appointed by the Central Government
- Composition: Maximum of **15 members** including:
  - A full-time **Chairperson**
  - Members representing Central Government Ministries
  - Representatives from State Pollution Control Boards
  - Persons with special knowledge in environmental matters

#### B. State Pollution Control Boards (SPCB) — Section 4
- Constituted by the **State Government** for each state
- Composition: Up to **15 members** including:
  - **Chairperson** (full-time, appointed by State Government)
  - Representatives of State Government departments
  - Local authority representatives
  - Persons with special knowledge

#### C. Joint Boards — Section 5
- Constituted by **two or more state governments** with consent of Central Government
- Deals with inter-state river/water pollution disputes
- Example: **Rajasthan & Punjab** have constituted joint boards for shared rivers

### 2.2 Functions of Central Pollution Control Board (Section 12)

The CPCB performs the following key functions:

1. **Advise the Central Government** on matters relating to prevention and control of water pollution
2. **Coordinate the activities** of State Boards and resolve disputes among them
3. **Lay down standards** for the quality of water (in consultation with State Boards)
4. **Lay down standards** for effluent discharge into water bodies
5. **Collect and disseminate** information relating to water pollution
6. **Plan and execute** nationwide programmes for prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution
7. **Coordinate with international agencies** on matters relating to water pollution

### 2.3 Functions of State Pollution Control Boards (Section 17)

The SPCBs perform the following functions:

1. **Plan programmes** for prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution in the state
2. **Advise the State Government** on water quality standards and effluent regulations
3. **Encourage, conduct, and participate** in investigations and research on water pollution
4. **Inspect sewage/trade effluent treatment plants** and review plans/specifications
5. **Evolve efficient methods** for treatment of sewage and trade effluents
6. **Lay down effluent standards** for industries after consulting CPCB
7. **Grant or refuse consent** for establishment of industries discharging effluents

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Sections of Water Act, 1974

| Section | Provision / Topic | Key Takeaway for Exam |
|:--------|:------------------|:----------------------|
| **Section 2** | Definitions | Pollutant, Stream, Occupier, Trade Effluent, Sewage Effluent |
| **Section 3** | Constitution of CPCB | Central Govt. sets up CPCB with max 15 members |
| **Section 4** | Constitution of SPCB | State Govt. sets up SPCB with max 15 members |
| **Section 5** | Joint Board | For 2+ states on inter-state water disputes |
| **Section 12** | Functions of CPCB | Lays down water quality and effluent standards |
| **Section 17** | Functions of SPCB | State-level planning and consent granting |
| **Section 19** | Power to restrict new outlets | State Govt. can prohibit establishment of industries in certain areas |
| **Section 20** | Power to give directions | SPCB can direct closure/regulation of polluting units |
| **Section 21** | Right to take samples | Officers can take samples for analysis |
| **Section 22** | Right to enter and inspect | Officers can enter any premises for inspection |
| **Section 23** | Power to obtain information | SPCB may require information from any person |
| **Section 24** | Prohibition on disposal | No person shall knowingly cause/permit polluting matter to enter stream/well |
| **Section 25** | Power of Board to take direct action | May apply to court for restraining pollution |
| **Section 26** | Power of Central Govt. to give directions | Directions to State Govt. for execution of the Act |
| **Section 27-28** | Appeal Provisions | Appeal to Tribunal/Court within 30 days |
| **Section 41-49** | Penalties | Fine up to ₹1 lakh + ₹5,000/day continuing; imprisonment up to 3 months |
| **Section 63** | Offences by Companies | Directors/Managers liable if offence committed with their consent |

### 2.5 Powers of State Pollution Control Boards (Sections 20, 21, 22, 23)

The SPCBs are vested with substantial powers to enforce the provisions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Enforcement Powers:**
> - **Investigative Power:** Right to enter any place, take samples, inspect installations
> - **Directive Power:** Order closure, prohibition, or regulation of any discharge
> - **Quasi-Judicial Power:** Hear complaints and impose penalties
> - **Consent Power:** Mandatory for industries under **Section 25** to obtain consent before establishment

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) and Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs) must comply with SPCB consent conditions
- **Chemical Engineering:** Discharge standards for chemical factories (pH, BOD, COD, TSS) must be met
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Effluent cooling systems and zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD) systems design
- **Environmental Engineering:** Water quality monitoring, river basin management
- **Project Management:** EIA (Environment Impact Assessment) studies and environmental clearances
- **Legal Compliance:** Every industry must obtain **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from the SPCB
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis of Key Provisions (Analytical Matrix)

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: Section 24 — Prohibition on Use of Stream or Well for Disposal of Polluting Matter

This is the **most critical penal provision** of the Water Act, 1974. Let us analyze it step by step:

**Step 1: Subject Matter of the Section**
- Section 24(1) prohibits **any person** from knowingly causing or permitting any **polluting matter** to enter into any **stream or well**.

**Step 2: Definition of "Stream" (already covered in Section 2)**
- Stream includes river, water course, inland water, well, sea, tidal water, estuary, canal, lake, pond, spring, reservoir — essentially **any body of water**.

**Step 3: Definition of "Polluting Matter" (Rule 2 of Water Act Rules, 1975)**
- Polluting matter includes:
  - Sewage, trade effluents, industrial waste
  - Solid waste, sludge, garbage
  - Residues from agriculture, construction, hospitals
  - Radioactive substances
  - Any other noxious substance

**Step 4: Mental Element (Mens Rea)**
- The section requires the act to be done **"knowingly"**
- Two scenarios: (a) actual knowledge of pollution, or (b) willful blindness to consequences

**Step 5: The Exception Clause**
- Subject to the **exemption granted under Section 25** — i.e., a person who has obtained prior consent of the SPCB is not in violation.

**Step 6: Penalty for Violation**
- Penalty under **Section 41**: Imprisonment up to **3 months** OR fine up to **₹1 lakh** OR both
- For **continuing offence**: Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** after the date of conviction
- For **failure to comply with Section 20 direction**: Up to **6 months imprisonment** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**

### 3.2 Exhaustive Analysis: Section 25 — Power of State Board to Make Application to Court for Restraining Pollution

**Step 1: Power Conferred**
- The SPCB may make an application to the **District Court** or any court of competent jurisdiction for restraining any person from causing pollution of water in a stream or well.

**Step 2: Conditions for Application**
- The Board must be of the opinion that a person is **likely to cause** or has already caused pollution.
- The application is to be made **after giving the person an opportunity of being heard**.

**Step 3: Powers of the Court**
- The court may pass orders including:
  - Order of **injunction** restraining the polluting act
  - **Mandatory injunction** to remove the polluting cause
  - Award of **damages** to the affected parties
  - Direction for **installation of treatment plants**

**Step 4: Application to State Government**
- The SPCB may also apply to the State Government for directions under **Section 19** to prohibit the establishment of industries in certain areas.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Analysis: Sections 19 & 20 — Power of State Government to Restrict Establishment

**Section 19 Analysis:**

$$\text{Section 19} = \text{State Government's Power to Declare Areas of Restricted Industry}$$

The State Government, in consultation with the SPCB, may:
- Declare certain areas as **"Prohibited Areas"** where no new outlet/inlet for discharge of sewage/trade effluent can be established.
- Declare certain areas as **"Restricted Areas"** where such outlets may be established only with prior permission.

**Section 20 Analysis:**

The SPCB may give **directions** to any person, including:
- Closing down the industry
- Installing treatment plants
- Modifying processes to reduce pollution
- Discontinuation of water supply to the industry
- Such other directions as it may deem necessary

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Flow of Regulatory Action

| Step | Authority | Action | Legal Basis |
|:-----|:----------|:-------|:------------|
| 1 | Industry | Apply for Consent to Establish (CTE) | Section 25(1) |
| 2 | SPCB | Grants or Refuses CTE | Section 25(1) |
| 3 | Industry | Establishes plant with conditions | Conditions of CTE |
| 4 | Industry | Applies for Consent to Operate (CTO) | Section 25(1) |
| 5 | SPCB | Grants CTO with effluent limits | Standards under Section 17 |
| 6 | SPCB Officer | Inspects premises | Section 22 |
| 7 | SPCB Officer | Takes samples for analysis | Section 21 |
| 8 | SPCB | Issues direction if violation found | Section 20 |
| 9 | If non-compliance | SPCB applies to Court | Section 25 |
| 10 | Court | Passes injunction order | Court powers |

### 3.5 Exhaustive Penalty Analysis (Sections 41-49)

**Section 41 — Penalty for Contravention of Section 24:**
- Imprisonment up to **3 months**, OR
- Fine up to **₹1 lakh**, OR
- Both
- **Continuing offence**: Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day**

**Section 42 — Penalty for Contravention of Section 22(2) (Refusal of Entry):**
- Imprisonment up to **3 months**, OR
- Fine up to **₹10,000**, OR
- Both

**Section 43 — Penalty for Failure to Comply with Directions (Section 20):**
- Imprisonment up to **6 months**, OR
- Fine up to **₹1 lakh**, OR
- Both
- Continuing offence: Additional **₹5,000 per day**

**Section 44 — Penalty for Damage to Water Bodies (Section 24):**
- Fine up to **₹1 lakh** (in addition to actual damages)

**Section 49 — Offences by Companies:**
- If offence is committed by a company, every person who at the time of offence was:
  - In charge of, or responsible to, the company for the conduct of business
  - **AND** the offence was committed with their consent/connivance/negligence
  - Shall be deemed guilty and liable to prosecution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defence for Company Officials (Section 49(2)):** A person can escape liability if he proves that the offence was committed **without his knowledge** or that he had exercised **all due diligence** to prevent the commission of such offence.

### 3.6 The Doctrine of Strict Liability in Water Pollution

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Principle:** Under the Water Act, the liability is **absolute** — once polluting matter enters a stream/well in violation of the standards, the offender is liable regardless of intent or negligence. This is akin to the **Rylands v. Fletcher** principle in tort law, applied to environmental offences.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Organizational Hierarchy of Pollution Control Boards

```mermaid

    CG[Central Government]
    SG1[State Government A]
    SG2[State Government B]
    SG3[State Government C]

    SPCB2[State Pollution Control Board SPCB B]
    SPCB3[State Pollution Control Board SPCB C]
    JB[Joint Board - Inter-state]
    INDUSTRY1[Industry Unit A]
    INDUSTRY2[Industry Unit B]
    CITIZEN[Citizens and NGOs]

    CG -->|Section 3| CPCB
    SG1 -->|Section 4| SPCB1
    SG2 -->|Section 4| SPCB2
    SG3 -->|Section 4| SPCB3
    SG1 -.->|Section 5| JB
    SG2 -.->|Section 5| JB
    CPCB -->|Coordinates - Section 12| SPCB1
    CPCB -->|Coordinates - Section 12| SPCB2
    CPCB -->|Coordinates - Section 12| SPCB3
    SPCB1 -->|Grants Consent CTE CTO| INDUSTRY1
    SPCB2 -->|Grants Consent CTE CTO| INDUSTRY2
    CITIZEN -->|Complaint| SPCB1
    CITIZEN -->|Complaint| CPCB
    JB -->|Inter-state Disputes| SPCB1
    JB -->|Inter-state Disputes| SPCB2
```

### 4.2 Compliance Process Flow for Industries under Water Act, 1974

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Industry Plans to Setup] --> B[Apply for Consent to Establish CTE under Section 25]
    B --> C{SPCB Reviews Application}
    C -->|Granted| D[Establish Plant with Effluent Treatment Plant ETP]
    C -->|Refused| Z[Appeal to Appellate Authority under Section 28]
    D --> E[Apply for Consent to Operate CTO]
    E --> F{SPCB Inspects and Verifies}
    F -->|Compliant| G[Grant of CTO - Period up to 5 years]
    F -->|Non-compliant| H[Direction under Section 20]
    H --> I{Complied}
    I -->|Yes| G
    I -->|No| J[SPCB Applies to Court under Section 25]
    J --> K[Court Issues Injunction or Closure Order]
    G --> L[Periodic Self-Monitoring and SPCB Inspection]
    L --> M{Violation Detected}
    M -->|Yes| H
    M -->|No| N[Continue Operations and Renewal]
    N --> G
    K --> O[Penalty Imprisonment under Section 41-44]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Enforcement Powers of SPCB

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SPCB[State Pollution Control Board SPCB]
    
    P1[Investigative Power Section 21-22]
    P2[Directive Power Section 20]
    P3[Consent Granting Power Section 25]
    P4[Quasi-Judicial Power]
    P5[Advisory Power Section 17]
    
    F1[Take Samples of Effluents]
    F2[Enter and Inspect Premises]
    F3[Examine Records and Documents]
    F4[Seize Equipment causing pollution]
    
    D1[Order Closure of Industry]
    D2[Order Installation of ETP]
    D3[Order Modification of Process]
    D4[Disconnect Water Supply]
    
    C1[Grant Consent to Establish CTE]
    C2[Grant Consent to Operate CTO]
    C3[Refuse Consent]
    C4[Revoke Consent]
    
    SPCB --> P1
    SPCB --> P2
    SPCB --> P3
    SPCB --> P4
    SPCB --> P5
    
    P1 --> F1
    P1 --> F2
    P1 --> F3
    P1 --> F4
    
    P2 --> D1
    P2 --> D2
    P2 --> D3
    P2 --> D4
    
    P3 --> C1
    P3 --> C2
    P3 --> C3
    P3 --> C4
```

### 4.4 Penalty Matrix Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OFF[Offence Type]
    
    OFF --> S24[Violation of Section 24 - Disposal of Polluting Matter]
    OFF --> S22[Refusal of Entry under Section 22]
    OFF --> S20[Non-compliance with Directions under Section 20]
    OFF --> S25[Operating without Consent under Section 25]
    
    S24 --> P41[Penalty Section 41]
    S22 --> P42[Penalty Section 42]
    S20 --> P43[Penalty Section 43]
    S25 --> P49[Penalty Section 49 read with Section 44]
    
    P41 --> T1[Imprisonment up to 3 months OR Fine up to Rs 1 lakh OR Both]
    P42 --> T2[Imprisonment up to 3 months OR Fine up to Rs 10000 OR Both]
    P43 --> T3[Imprisonment up to 6 months OR Fine up to Rs 1 lakh OR Both]
    P49 --> T4[Director Manager Officer Liable Imprisonment and Fine]
    
    T1 --> C1[Continuing Offence Additional Rs 5000 per day]
    T2 --> C1
    T3 --> C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Define the term 'Water Pollution' as per Section 2(e) of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
According to **Section 2(e)** of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974, "water pollution" means such contamination of water or such change in its physical, chemical or biological properties, or such discharge of any sewage, or trade effluent, or of any other liquid, gaseous or solid substance into water, as may, or is likely to, create a nuisance or render such water harmful or injurious to public health or safety, or to domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural or other legitimate uses, or to the life and health of animals or plants or of aquatic organisms. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**"What is a 'Joint Board' under the Water Act, 1974? When is it constituted?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Joint Board** is constituted under **Section 5** of the Water Act, 1974, by an agreement between **two or more State Governments** (with the consent of the Central Government) for the prevention and control of water pollution in inter-state rivers or shared water bodies. The Joint Board has the same powers and functions as a State Pollution Control Board but operates over a region spanning multiple states. Example: Joint Board constituted for the river Yamuna between Delhi and Uttar Pradesh. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a) Explain in detail the composition and functions of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** is constituted by the Central Government under **Section 3(1)** of the Water Act, 1974. The CPCB is headquartered in **Delhi** and is the apex regulatory body for water pollution control in India.

**Composition (Section 3):**
- A full-time **Chairperson** appointed by the Central Government
- Maximum of **15 members** in total
- The members include:
  - Officials representing the Central Government dealing with health, environment, and water resources
  - Representatives from the State Pollution Control Boards
  - Persons having special knowledge or practical experience in matters relating to environmental protection, water pollution, and health

**Functions of CPCB (Section 12):**
1. **Advisory Function:** Advise the Central Government on any matter concerning the prevention and control of water pollution.
2. **Coordination Function:** Coordinate the activities of the State Pollution Control Boards and resolve disputes among them.
3. **Standard-Setting Function:** Lay down standards for the **quality of water** in various water bodies for different uses (drinking, bathing, industrial cooling, etc.).
4. **Effluent Standards:** Lay down standards for the discharge of effluents from industries and other sources.
5. **Information Function:** Collect and disseminate information relating to water pollution and its prevention.
6. **Programme Function:** Plan and execute nationwide programmes for the prevention, control, and abatement of water pollution.
7. **Research Function:** Encourage, conduct, and participate in investigations and research on water pollution.
8. **International Cooperation:** Coordinate with international agencies on matters relating to water pollution control.

The CPCB plays a pivotal role as the national-level technical and regulatory authority, ensuring uniform standards and inter-state coordination. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Discuss the powers of the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) under the Water Act, 1974, with reference to the consent mechanism. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** is constituted under **Section 4** of the Water Act, 1974, by the respective State Governments. The SPCB is the primary enforcement authority at the state level for water pollution control.

**Powers of SPCB:**

**1. Investigative Powers (Section 21 and 22):**
- **Right to Take Samples:** Officers of the SPCB can take samples of effluents from any industrial unit for analysis.
- **Right to Enter and Inspect:** The SPCB can enter any premises at all reasonable times for inspection.
- **Right to Examine:** The SPCB can examine records, registers, documents, and the manufacturing process.

**2. Directive Powers (Section 20):**
The SPCB may give directions to any person, including:
- **Closure** of the polluting industry
- **Installation** of effluent treatment plants (ETPs)
- **Modification** of industrial processes to reduce pollution
- **Discontinuation** of water supply to the polluting unit
- Any other direction deemed necessary

**3. Consent Mechanism (Section 25) — Most Important Power:**
Under Section 25(1), no person shall:
- Establish any **new outlet** for discharge of sewage or trade effluent into a stream/well, OR
- Begin to make any **new discharge** of sewage or trade effluent,
without the **prior consent of the State Pollution Control Board**.

**Types of Consent:**
- **Consent to Establish (CTE):** Required before setting up the industry — ensures the design includes proper effluent treatment.
- **Consent to Operate (CTO):** Required before commencing operations — verifies that the ETP is functional and compliant.

**4. Quasi-Judicial Powers:**
The SPCB has the power to:
- Hear complaints from citizens
- Pass orders for compliance
- Apply to courts for injunctions

**5. Power to Apply to Court (Section 25):**
The SPCB may apply to the **District Court** or any court of competent jurisdiction for restraining pollution.

The SPCB's powers are comprehensive and ensure that water pollution is regulated at the grassroots level in every state. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**(a) Explain the salient features and objectives of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Water Act, 1974, is the cornerstone of Indian water pollution legislation. The salient features and objectives are as follows:

**Objectives of the Act:**
1. **Primary Objective:** Prevention and control of water pollution and maintaining or restoring the **wholesomeness of water** in India.
2. **Establishment of Boards:** Setting up of Central and State Pollution Control Boards to carry out the purposes of the Act.
3. **Standard Setting:** Prescribing water quality standards and effluent discharge standards.
4. **Penal Provisions:** Providing penalties for contravention of the Act's provisions.

**Salient Features:**

1. **Comprehensive Coverage:** The Act applies to all water bodies — rivers, lakes, ponds, wells, sea, tidal waters, and estuaries.

2. **Three-Tier Regulatory Structure:**
   - Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)
   - State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)
   - Joint Boards for inter-state rivers

3. **Preventive and Curative Approach:** The Act emphasizes prevention through the consent mechanism, with penalties for violation.

4. **Consent Mechanism:** Industries must obtain **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from the SPCB under Section 25.

5. **Right to Sample and Inspect:** Officers of the Board have wide powers to enter, inspect, and take samples.

6. **Power to Give Directions:** Boards can issue directions under Section 20 to control pollution.

7. **Penal Provisions:** Detailed penalty provisions under Sections 41-49, including imprisonment up to **3 months/6 months** and fines up to **₹1 lakh** with additional **₹5,000 per day** for continuing offences.

8. **Liability of Companies:** Under Section 49, directors, managers, and officers of companies are personally liable for offences committed with their consent or connivance.

9. **Citizen Participation:** Any person aggrieved by water pollution may file a complaint, ensuring public participation.

10. **Constitutional Validity:** The Act is constitutionally backed by **Article 252(1)** and has been adopted by all states.

The Act represents a holistic framework for addressing water pollution in India. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Discuss in detail the penalty provisions of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Water Act, 1974, contains detailed penalty provisions under **Sections 41-49** to ensure compliance:

**1. Section 41 — Penalty for Contravention of Section 24:**
- Applicable to any person who contravenes the provisions of Section 24 (disposal of polluting matter into stream/well).
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 months**, OR fine up to **₹1 lakh**, OR both.
- **Continuing Offence:** Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** during the continuation of the offence after conviction.

**2. Section 42 — Penalty for Refusal of Entry:**
- Applicable to any person who refuses entry to an authorized officer of the SPCB under Section 22(2).
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 months**, OR fine up to **₹10,000**, OR both.

**3. Section 43 — Penalty for Failure to Comply with Directions:**
- Applicable when a person fails to comply with the directions issued by the SPCB under Section 20.
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **6 months**, OR fine up to **₹1 lakh**, OR both.
- **Continuing Offence:** Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day**.

**4. Section 44 — Penalty for Damage to Water Bodies:**
- Applicable for damage caused to any water body.
- **Punishment:** Fine up to **₹1 lakh**, in addition to actual damages.

**5. Section 49 — Offences by Companies:**
- Every person who at the time of offence was in charge of, or responsible to, the company for the conduct of the business, is deemed guilty.
- The offence must have been committed with their **consent, connivance, or negligence**.
- **Defence:** Such person can escape liability by proving that the offence was committed **without their knowledge** or that they exercised **all due diligence** to prevent it.

**6. Cognizance of Offences (Section 47):**
- No court shall take cognizance of any offence under the Act except on a complaint made by:
  - The Board, OR
  - Any person authorized by the Board, OR
  - With the previous sanction of the Board.

**7. Trial of Offences:**
- Offences are tried by a Magistrate of the First Class or higher.
- The penalty structure is designed to be **deterrent and proportionate** to the harm caused.

The penalty provisions ensure strict enforcement and act as a strong deterrent against water pollution. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CPCB and SPCB Functions:** Students often mix up the functions. Remember: **CPCB = National level + Standard setting**, **SPCB = State level + Consent granting + Enforcement**.
>
> 2. **Wrong Section Numbers:** Many students cite wrong sections (e.g., writing Section 20 for consent instead of Section 25). Memorize: **Consent = Section 25**, **Directions = Section 20**, **Penalty = Sections 41-49**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting "Continuing Offence" Provision:** When asked about penalties, always mention the **₹5,000 per day additional fine** for continuing offences — this is a high-yield point.
>
> 4. **Not Mentioning Mens Rea in Section 24:** Section 24 requires the act to be done **"knowingly"** — this is the mental element. Don't miss it.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Joint Boards:** When asked about regulatory bodies, always mention **all three** — CPCB, SPCB, and Joint Boards.
>
> 6. **Confusing Water Act with Air Act:** Don't interchange provisions. Water Act, 1974 deals with **water bodies**; Air Act, 1981 deals with **air pollution**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Article 252(1):** When asked about the constitutional basis, always mention **Article 252(1)** of the Constitution.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Act Name:** Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, **1974**
> - **Constitutional Basis:** Article **252(1)** of the Indian Constitution
> - **CPCB:** Constituted under **Section 3**, headquartered in **Delhi**, max **15 members**
> - **SPCB:** Constituted under **Section 4** by State Governments, max **15 members**
> - **Joint Board:** Constituted under **Section 5** for inter-state rivers
> - **CPCB Functions:** Section **12** — advisory, coordination, standard setting, research
> - **SPCB Functions:** Section **17** — state-level planning, consent, enforcement
> - **Powers of SPCB:** Sections **20, 21, 22, 23, 25** — directive, investigative, consent
> - **Consent Mechanism:** **Section 25** — CTE and CTO mandatory
> - **Penalty for Section 24 Violation:** **Section 41** — 3 months imprisonment OR ₹1 lakh fine
> - **Penalty for Section 20 Violation:** **Section 43** — 6 months imprisonment OR ₹1 lakh fine
> - **Continuing Offence:** **₹5,000 per day** additional fine
> - **Company Liability:** **Section 49** — directors and managers personally liable
> - **Definition of Stream:** Includes river, well, lake, sea, tidal water, etc. (broad definition)
> - **Key Case:** **M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak case)** — relevant for environmental jurisprudence
> - **Amendment:** Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Amendment Act, **1988** — strengthened the Act and renamed Water Cess Act, 1977 as Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act
> - **Related Acts:** Water Cess Act **1977** (for water cess), EPA Act **1986** (umbrella environmental law)

**All the best for your KTU 2024 examinations!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power to Make Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT411 - LEGAL ASPECTS OF HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power to Make Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Health, Safety, and Environment (HSE) law in India, the **"Power to Make Rules"** refers to the statutory authority conferred upon the **Central Government** and/or **State Government** (and in some cases, prescribed authorities) by the parent legislation to frame, amend, and notify subordinate or delegated legislation for the effective implementation of the substantive provisions of the law. This power is known in legal parlance as the power of **subordinate legislation** or **delegated legislation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> The "Power to Make Rules" is the legislative competence delegated by the Parliament or State Legislature to the Executive (Government) to formulate detailed rules, regulations, and bye-laws necessary to operationalize the broad framework of a parent Act, such as the Factories Act, 1948, the Mines Act, 1952, the Dock Workers (Safety, Health and Welfare) Act, 1986, and the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions (OSH) Code, 2020.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Indian Constitution** as the *master blueprint* of a huge building. The **Parliament** passes the *parent Act* (e.g., the Factories Act), which lays down the broad structural pillars — saying *where* walls must be built and *why* a staircase is needed. However, the parent Act cannot possibly specify *every nut and bolt, every paint shade, and every wire* that the building needs.

So, Parliament hands over a *delegated toolbox* to the **Executive (Central/State Government)** and says: *"You fill in the technical details — the exact dimensions, the safety wire thickness, the fire-exit signage, the air-quality testing frequency."* This toolbox is the **Power to Make Rules**.

**Real-world analogy — Driving Rules:**
- The **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** is the parent law.
- The detailed rules on speed limits in school zones, the format of license plates, and emission test procedures are made by the Government using the **Power to Make Rules**.

In HSE law, the parent Acts (Factories Act, OSH Code) set the *goals* (safe workplace, healthy workers, clean environment), and the **Rules** prescribe the *methods* (ventilation standards in $\text{m}^3$ per worker, fencing of machinery, personal protective equipment specifications).

## 1.3 Constitutional Foundation

The power to make rules derives its legitimacy from the **Constitution of India**:
- **Article 246** read with **Schedule VII, List III (Concurrent List)**, Entries **22** (Trade unions; industrial and labour disputes), **24** (Welfare of labour including conditions of work, provident funds, employers' liability, workmen's compensation, invalidity and old age pensions, maternity benefits), and **Entry 29** (Prevention of the extension from one State to another of infectious or contagious diseases or pests affecting men, animals or plants).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point for KTU Exam:** Since HSE matters fall under the **Concurrent List**, both Parliament and State Legislatures can legislate. However, in case of repugnancy under **Article 254**, the Central law prevails. This is why the *power to make rules* is split between the **Central Government** and **State Government** in most HSE statutes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Nature and Scope of the Power

The power to make rules is **NOT absolute**. It is a **conditional, ancillary, and subordinate** power. The classical statement of the law is from the celebrated Supreme Court judgment in **J.K. Gas Plant v. State of Madhya Pradesh (1977)**, where the Court held that the power of delegated legislation must be exercised **within the four corners of the parent Act**.

### 2.1.1 Salient Principles Governing Rule-Making Power

1. **Principle of Ultravires** — Rules that go beyond the parent Act (or its preamble) are *ultra vires* and void. The rules must be *intra vires* the parent statute.
2. **Principle of Substantive Ultra Vires** — If the rules violate **Article 14** (equality), **Article 19** (reasonable restrictions on fundamental rights), or **Article 21** (life and personal liberty), they can be struck down.
3. **Principle of Procedural Ultra Vires** — Failure to comply with the procedural requirements (consultation, publication, laying) renders rules invalid.
4. **Principle of Reasonableness** — Rules imposing restrictions on fundamental rights must satisfy the test of reasonableness under **Article 19(6)** and **Article 14**.
5. **Principle of Non-Delegation** — The essential legislative function cannot be delegated (*Delegatus non potest delegare*). However, *supplementary* and *ancillary* powers can be delegated.

## 2.2 Power to Make Rules under the Factories Act, 1948

The Factories Act, 1948 contains explicit and detailed provisions for rule-making.

### 2.2.1 Section 112 — State Government's Power

> **Section 112(1):** The State Government may make rules to carry out the purposes of this Act.

The scope of rules under Section 112 includes:
- Prescribing fees for licenses, registration, and approvals
- Specifying qualifications of persons in charge of factories
- Laying down standards for ventilation, temperature, humidity, lighting, drainage
- Procedures for the registration of factories
- The form of notices and applications
- Maintenance of registers and records

### 2.2.2 Section 113 — Central Government's Power

> **Section 113:** The Central Government may, after consultation with the State Government, make rules prescribing standards of ventilation, temperature, humidity, lighting, and other matters relating to safety and welfare of workers in factories.

The Central Government's power is **residuary and harmonizing** in nature. It is meant to ensure **uniformity** of standards across all States.

### 2.2.3 Section 114 — Publication of Rules

- All rules made under the Act must be **published in the Official Gazette**.
- Publication is a **mandatory condition precedent** — *non-publication makes the rule non-est (not a rule)*. This was held in **D.K. Krishnan v. State of Tamil Nadu (1978)**.

### 2.2.4 Section 115 — Laying of Rules

- All rules made under the Act shall be **laid before the State Legislature** (for State rules) or **Parliament** (for Central rules) for a total period of **30 days**.
- The Legislature can modify, annul, or amend the rules.
- Laying is a **constitutional safeguard** ensuring legislative oversight over delegated legislation.

## 2.3 Power to Make Rules under the OSH Code, 2020

The Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 (Section 156–159) consolidates rule-making powers:

| Section | Authority | Scope |
|:--------|:----------|:------|
| Section 156 | State Government | To carry out the purposes of the Code at the State level |
| Section 157 | Central Government | To remove difficulties, harmonize standards, prescribe additional safety norms |
| Section 158 | Publication | All rules to be published in Official Gazette and on a website |
| Section 159 | Laying | To be laid before State Legislature / Parliament |

## 2.4 Power to Make Rules under the Mines Act, 1952

- **Section 22** empowers the Central Government to make regulations for every mine.
- The **Director-General of Mines Safety (DGMS)** frames detailed **Regulations, Circulars, and Standing Orders** for safety standards in mines.
- These subsidiary instruments are subordinate to the parent Act and have the force of law.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Provisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary Table for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| Statute | Section | Authority | Purpose | Laying |
|:--------|:--------|:----------|:--------|:-------|
| Factories Act, 1948 | S. 112 | State Govt. | Implementation of the Act | Before State Legislature |
| Factories Act, 1948 | S. 113 | Central Govt. | Uniform standards | Before Parliament |
| Factories Act, 1948 | S. 114 | All | Publication in Official Gazette | — |
| Factories Act, 1948 | S. 115 | All | Laying procedure | 30 days session |
| Mines Act, 1952 | S. 22 | Central Govt. | Framing of Regulations | Before Parliament |
| Dock Workers Act, 1986 | S. 17 | Central/State Govt. | Safety and welfare rules | Before respective Legislature |
| OSH Code, 2020 | S. 156 | State Govt. | Operational rules | Before State Legislature |
| OSH Code, 2020 | S. 157 | Central Govt. | Harmonizing standards | Before Parliament |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

For an HSE professional or engineer, understanding the "Power to Make Rules" is critical because:

1. **Statutory Compliance Audits** — Every factory must comply not only with the parent Act but also with the *Rules* framed thereunder. For example, the **Tamil Nadu Factories Rules, 1950** specify the exact $\text{cubic metres}$ of ventilation per worker.
2. **Safety Standards Application** — The Standards prescribed by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, made applicable through Rules, become enforceable in workplaces.
3. **Penalty Calculation** — The quantum of fines and penalties in many cases is specified in the **Schedules of the Rules**, not the parent Act.
4. **International Trade** — Indian Rules must align with international conventions like **ILO Convention No. 155** (Occupational Safety and Health). Rules that incorporate international standards enable better trade integration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Procedural Derivation

Since this topic is **legal-procedural** in nature, the "derivation" here is a **logical step-by-step analysis** of how a rule is conceived, drafted, notified, and challenged. This framework is **essential for KTU long-answer questions**.

## 3.1 The Rule-Making Process — Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

### Step 1: Identification of Legislative Gap
The process begins when the Government (or a delegated authority) identifies that the parent Act contains a **broad provision** that requires **operational detailing** for effective enforcement.

> *Example:* The Factories Act, Section 14 mandates *"effective and suitable provision for securing and maintaining in every work-room adequate ventilation."* The Act does not specify *how many cubic metres of air* are required. This gap is identified.

### Step 2: Initiation and Consultative Drafting
The appropriate Government department (e.g., Department of Industrial Policy, Ministry of Labour and Employment) initiates the drafting process.

$$
\text{Parent Act (Broad Goal)} \xrightarrow{\text{Gap Identified}} \text{Departmental Draft} \xrightarrow{\text{Consultation}} \text{Final Draft}
$$

The draft may be circulated to:
- **Stakeholders** (industry associations, trade unions)
- **Expert bodies** (DGMS, BIS, Indian Association of Occupational Health)
- **Legal vetting** (Legislative Department of the Ministry of Law and Justice)

### Step 3: Pre-Publication (Where Mandatory)
Some statutes require the draft rules to be **published in the Official Gazette** inviting objections/suggestions from the public within a specified period (often 30 to 60 days).

> This is a **principle of natural justice** embedded in rule-making.

### Step 4: Finalization and Approval
After considering the objections, the **Appropriate Government** (Central or State) approves the final draft. The Cabinet / Council of Ministers or the competent minister authorizes notification.

### Step 5: Notification in the Official Gazette
The rules are **published in the Official Gazette** under the relevant section of the parent Act. Publication is **mandatory and constitutive** of the rule.

> **Legal Maxim:** *"A rule is not a rule until it is published."*

### Step 6: Laying Before the Legislature
The rules are laid before:
- **Parliament** (for Central Government rules)
- **State Legislative Assembly** (for State Government rules)

The Legislature can:
- **Modify** the rules
- **Annul** the rules (cancel them)
- **Approve** them as they are

The laying period is typically **30 days** (continuous or in two sessions).

### Step 7: Commencement
The rules specify their own **date of commencement**. If no date is specified, they come into force on the **date of publication** in the Official Gazette.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Grounds of Judicial Review of Rules

The following table maps the **legal grounds** on which a Rule can be challenged in a Court of Law. This is a high-frequency KTU question area.

| S. No. | Ground of Challenge | Legal Basis | Judicial Test | Leading Case |
|:------:|:-------------------|:------------|:--------------|:-------------|
| 1 | Ultra vires the parent Act | Section 112/113 of Factories Act | Does the rule travel beyond the parent Act? | *J.K. Gas Plant v. State of MP* (1977) |
| 2 | Substantive ultra vires — Article 14 | Doctrine of Non-Arbitrariness | Is the rule discriminatory or arbitrary? | *Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India* (1978) |
| 3 | Substantive ultra vires — Article 19 | Reasonable Restrictions | Is the restriction in the interest of the public? | *Chintaman Rao v. State of MP* (1958) |
| 4 | Procedural ultra vires | Section 114 (Publication) | Was the rule published in the Official Gazette? | *D.K. Krishnan v. State of TN* (1978) |
| 5 | Lack of consultation | Statutory requirement | Was consultation mandatory and complied with? | *Raza Buland Sugar Co. v. Rampur* (1973) |
| 6 | Sub-delegation | Doctrine of Non-Delegation | Has essential legislative function been re-delegated? | *D.S. Garewal v. State of Punjab* (1959) |
| 7 | Manifest arbitrariness | Article 14, modern test | Is the rule so arbitrary that it shocks the conscience? | *Shayara Bano v. Union of India* (2017) |

## 3.3 Doctrine of Subordinate Legislation — Critical Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> **Balancing Act for KTU Exam:**
> The Indian Supreme Court has consistently recognized the **necessity of delegated legislation** because:
> - Parliament has limited time and technical expertise.
> - HSE matters require **technical and dynamic** rules (e.g., emission standards change with new technology).
> - Emergency situations (e.g., COVID-19 workplace safety rules) demand swift executive action.

However, the Court has also stressed that the **rule-making power is a trust** and any abuse invites judicial intervention.

## 3.4 Substantive and Procedural Safeguards — Summation

$$
\text{Rule Validity} = f(\text{Intra vires Parent Act}, \text{Constitutionality}, \text{Procedural Compliance}, \text{Reasonableness})
$$

Where:
- **Intra vires Parent Act** — Rule must not exceed parent Act's scope.
- **Constitutionality** — Rule must not violate Fundamental Rights.
- **Procedural Compliance** — Publication, consultation, laying.
- **Reasonableness** — Rule must not be arbitrary or discriminatory.

If any of the four conditions fails, the rule is **void ab initio** (void from the beginning).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Rule-Making Process under the Factories Act, 1948

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Act Identifies Gap] --> B[Departmental Drafting]
    B --> C[Consultation with Stakeholders]
    C --> D[Legal Vetting by Ministry of Law]
    D --> E[Pre-Publication in Official Gazette]
    E --> F{Public Objections?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Consider and Incorporate Changes]
    F -->|No| H[Final Approval by Council of Ministers]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Notification in Official Gazette]
    I --> J[Laying Before Parliament or State Legislature]
    J --> K{30 Days Laying Period}
    K -->|Modifications Suggested| L[Government Amends Rules]
    K -->|Approved| M[Rule Comes into Force]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Implementation and Inspection by Inspectorate]
    N --> O{Non-Compliance?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Prosecution under Section 92 of Factories Act]
    O -->|No| Q[Compliance Achieved]

    style A fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style M fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style P fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Rule-Making Authorities in HSE Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constitution of India Article 246 Schedule VII] --> B[Parliament Passes Parent Act]
    B --> C[Central Government Section 113]
    B --> D[State Government Section 112]
    C --> E[Central Rules Gazette Notified]
    D --> F[State Rules Gazette Notified]
    E --> G[Director General Factory Advice Service]
    E --> H[DGMS for Mines]
    E --> I[BIS Standards Referenced]
    F --> J[State Factory Inspectorate]
    F --> K[State Labour Commissioner]
    G --> L[Factory Level Compliance]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Workplace Safety and Health Achieved]

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style B fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    style L fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Judicial Review Mechanism for Rules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rule Notified in Gazette] --> B{Person Aggrieved}
    B -->|Yes| C[File Writ Petition under Article 226 or Article 32]
    B -->|No| D[Rule Operates and is Enforced]
    C --> E[High Court or Supreme Court]
    E --> F{Grounds of Challenge}
    F -->|Ultra vires Parent Act| G[Rule Quashed as Void]
    F -->|Violates Article 14| G
    F -->|Violates Article 19| G
    F -->|Procedural Non-Compliance| G
    F -->|Manifest Arbitrariness| G
    F -->|No Valid Ground| H[Petition Dismissed Rule Upheld]
    G --> I[Rule Struck Down]
    H --> D

    style G fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style I fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
    style H fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Constitutional Allocation of HSE Rule-Making Power

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constitution of India] --> B[Union List List I]
    A --> C[State List List II]
    A --> D[Concurrent List List III]
    D --> E1[Entry 22 Industrial Disputes]
    D --> E2[Entry 24 Labour Welfare]
    D --> E3[Entry 29 Prevention of Diseases]
    E1 --> F[Both Centre and State can Legislate]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Repugnancy under Article 254}
    G -->|Yes Central Prevails| H[Central Rules Apply]
    G -->|No Repugnancy| I[Both Rules Apply Harmoniously]
    H --> J[Factories Act 1948 Section 113]
    I --> K[Factories Act 1948 Section 112]

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style K fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q: Define the term "Power to Make Rules" in the context of the Factories Act, 1948. Mention the relevant sections.**

**Model Answer:**

The "Power to Make Rules" is the statutory authority conferred upon the Central Government under **Section 113** and the State Government under **Section 112** of the Factories Act, 1948, to frame subordinate or delegated legislation for implementing the purposes of the Act. The Central Government frames rules prescribing **uniform standards** for ventilation, temperature, humidity, lighting, and safety after consultation with the State Government, while the State Government frames rules to **carry out the purposes of the Act** at the State level. These rules are subordinate to the parent Act and have the force of law once published in the Official Gazette under **Section 114** and laid before the Legislature under **Section 115**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: Why is publication of rules in the Official Gazette considered mandatory under the Factories Act, 1948?**

**Model Answer:**

Publication in the Official Gazette is **mandatory and constitutive** under **Section 114** of the Factories Act, 1948. The legal maxim *"a rule is not a rule until it is published"* applies. In **D.K. Krishnan v. State of Tamil Nadu (1978)**, the Supreme Court held that non-publication renders the rule a *nullity* because:
1. **Citizens must know the law** — Ignorance of law is no excuse, but this applies only to *published* law.
2. **Certainty and predictability** — Affected parties (employers, workers) need clear notice of obligations.
3. **Democracy and accountability** — Public notification enables scrutiny and objection.

Without publication, the rule has no legal force and cannot be enforced.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the power of the **State Government** to make rules under the **Factories Act, 1948**. Discuss the scope, limitations, and safeguards attached to this power. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **judicial review of subordinate legislation** with reference to the principles laid down by the Supreme Court of India. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — State Government's Power under the Factories Act, 1948:**

**1. Statutory Basis:**
Section 112(1) of the Factories Act, 1948 empowers the State Government to make rules to carry out the purposes of the Act. This power is exercisable after prior publication where the State Government considers it expedient.

**2. Scope of Section 112:**
The scope is very wide and includes rules on:
- The procedure for registration and grant of licenses
- Fees for licenses and renewal
- Qualifications of the occupier, manager, and persons in charge
- Standards for ventilation, temperature, humidity, lighting, drainage, and dust removal
- Maintenance of registers, records, and submission of returns
- The form of notices and applications
- Appointment of inspectors and their powers
- Health and welfare measures (drinking water, canteens, first-aid)
- Safety procedures for dangerous machinery and processes

**3. Limitations:**
- **Subordination to Parent Act** — Rules must be *intra vires* Section 112 and the Act as a whole. They cannot enlarge or modify the substantive provisions of the Act.
- **Constitutional Limitations** — Rules cannot violate Fundamental Rights (Articles 14, 19, 21).
- **Procedural Limitations** — Must comply with consultation requirements and publication under Section 114.

**4. Safeguards:**
- **Section 114** — Publication in the Official Gazette is mandatory.
- **Section 115** — Laying of rules before the State Legislature for 30 days. The Legislature can modify or annul.
- **Judicial Review** — Any person aggrieved can challenge the rules before the High Court under Article 226 or the Supreme Court under Article 32.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Judicial Review of Subordinate Legislation:**

**1. Meaning:**
Judicial review of subordinate legislation refers to the power of the superior courts (High Courts and the Supreme Court) to examine the **validity of rules, regulations, bye-laws, and notifications** made by the Executive under the authority of a parent Act.

**2. Grounds of Judicial Review:**

**(i) Substantive Ultra Vires:**
- The rule goes beyond the parent Act.
- *Case:* **J.K. Gas Plant v. State of MP (1977)** — Rule was struck down for travelling beyond the parent Act.

**(ii) Procedural Ultra Vires:**
- Failure to comply with mandatory procedural requirements.
- *Case:* **D.K. Krishnan v. State of TN (1978)** — Non-publication in Official Gazette rendered rule void.

**(iii) Violation of Article 14:**
- Rule is arbitrary, discriminatory, or unreasonable.
- *Case:* **Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)** — Expanded Article 14 to include arbitrariness.

**(iv) Violation of Article 19:**
- Rule imposes unreasonable restrictions on freedom of trade or occupation.
- *Case:* **Chintaman Rao v. State of MP (1958)** — Reasonable restriction must have a direct nexus to public interest.

**(v) Manifest Arbitrariness:**
- Rule shocks the conscience of the Court.
- *Case:* **Shayara Bano v. Union of India (2017)**.

**3. Safeguards and Conclusion:**
Judicial review ensures that the **executive does not become a despot** and that the rule-making power is exercised as a **trust**. The Indian approach strikes a balance between the need for **administrative flexibility** and the protection of **individual rights**.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Choice B]**

**(a)** Discuss the **constitutional basis** of the power to make rules in HSE law, with reference to the **Concurrent List** and **Article 254** of the Constitution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **process of rule-making** under the Factories Act, 1948, highlighting the importance of **publication** and **laying** before the Legislature. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Constitutional Basis of Rule-Making Power:**

**1. Legislative Competence:**
The Constitution of India under **Article 246** read with **Schedule VII** allocates legislative powers between the Union and the States through three lists:
- **List I (Union List)** — Subjects on which only Parliament can legislate.
- **List II (State List)** — Subjects on which only State Legislature can legislate.
- **List III (Concurrent List)** — Subjects on which **both** can legislate.

**2. HSE Subjects in the Concurrent List:**
Health, Safety, and Environment matters relevant to factories, mines, and workplaces are primarily covered under:
- **Entry 22** — Trade unions; industrial and labour disputes.
- **Entry 24** — Welfare of labour including conditions of work, employers' liability, workmen's compensation.
- **Entry 29** — Prevention of the extension from one State to another of infectious or contagious diseases or pests affecting men.

**3. Significance of Concurrent List:**
- Both Parliament and State Legislatures can enact laws on these subjects.
- In practice, Parliament enacts the **parent Act** (e.g., Factories Act, 1948), and the State Government exercises the **Power to Make Rules** under Sections 112, 113, etc.

**4. Article 254 — Doctrine of Repugnancy:**
Where there is a conflict between a Central law and a State law on a Concurrent subject, the **Central law prevails**, and the State law becomes void to the extent of repugnancy. However, if the State law has received Presidential assent, it can prevail in that State.

**5. Conclusion:**
The constitutional design ensures **uniform minimum standards** (Central) while allowing **regional adaptation** (State). The Power to Make Rules is a **constitutionally sanctioned device** for operationalizing legislative intent in a federal structure.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Process of Rule-Making under the Factories Act, 1948:**

**1. Initiation:**
The appropriate Government (Central or State) identifies the need for detailed rules to implement the Act.

**2. Drafting:**
The Department of Industrial Policy or the State Labour Department drafts the rules, often in consultation with:
- Industry associations (e.g., CII, FICCI)
- Trade unions (e.g., BMS, INTUC)
- Technical experts (DGMS, BIS)

**3. Pre-Publication (Optional but desirable):**
Draft rules may be published in the Official Gazette inviting objections from the public within a stipulated period (often 30 to 60 days).

**4. Finalization:**
The Government considers objections, makes modifications, and approves the final draft.

**5. Notification under Section 114:**
The final rules are **notified in the Official Gazette**. **Publication is mandatory** for the rule to have legal force (*D.K. Krishnan v. State of TN, 1978*).

**6. Laying before the Legislature under Section 115:**
The rules are **laid before the State Legislature or Parliament** for a total period of **30 days** during one or more sessions. The Legislature can:
- **Approve** the rules.
- **Modify** the rules.
- **Annul** the rules (render them void prospectively).

**7. Commencement:**
The rules specify their own date of commencement or, if not specified, come into force on the date of publication.

**8. Importance of Publication and Laying:**
- **Publication** ensures *fair notice* to citizens and prevents the Government from enforcing secret rules.
- **Laying** ensures *legislative oversight* and prevents the executive from assuming uncontrolled legislative power.
- Together, they form the **constitutional firewall** against executive absolutism.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Many students write that the State Government has *unlimited* power to make rules. **Wrong** — the power is *conditional, ancillary, and subordinate* to the parent Act.
> 2. **Citation Error:** Do NOT write "Section 112 of the Constitution" or "Article 112 of Factories Act." Article 112 relates to the **Budget**; Section 112 relates to the **Factories Act**.
> 3. **Missing Cases:** Always cite **D.K. Krishnan v. State of TN (1978)** for the publication requirement and **J.K. Gas Plant v. State of MP (1977)** for ultra vires.
