## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS WITH INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

**(a) Explain in detail the statutory framework under the Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC Rules) that mandates the disclosure of safety information to the public. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the role of the Right to Information Act, 2005 in empowering the public to demand safety information from hazardous industries. What are the penalties for non-disclosure? (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **MSIHC Rules, 1989** were framed under **Section 6, 8 and 25 of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. The relevant rules are:

**1. Applicability (1 Mark):** The Rules apply to any industrial activity involving the manufacture, storage, or import of hazardous chemicals listed in **Schedule I** (list of chemicals with threshold quantities) and **Schedule II** (list of categories of installations).

**2. Rule 13 — On-Site Emergency Plan (1.5 Marks):**
- The **occupier** must prepare an On-Site Emergency Plan.
- It must identify the hazards, the **worst-case scenario**, the **command structure**, and the **escape routes**.
- The Plan must be filed with the **District Emergency Authority** within **90 days** of the commencement of the activity.

**3. Rule 14 — Off-Site Emergency Plan (1.5 Marks):**
- Prepared by the **District Collector / District Magistrate** in consultation with the occupier.
- Covers the **public area** outside the factory boundary, up to a **5-km radius** for major hazard installations.
- Must be **reviewed every 6 months** and **tested annually** through mock drills with public participation.

**4. Rule 15 — Information to Public (1 Mark):**
- In the event of a major accident, the occupier must **immediately inform**:
  - The District Authority,
  - Workers and their representatives,
  - The public likely to be affected, and
  - The media (as per the Off-Site Plan).

**5. Rule 17 — Penalties (1 Mark):**
- Failure to comply → imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹10 lakhs** + **additional ₹5,000/day** for continuing offences.

**6. Rule 18 — Disclosure to Importer (1 Mark):**
- Any new chemical being imported must be **disclosed to the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)** before the import consignment arrives.

#### **Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. RTI Act 2005 — Genesis (1 Mark):**
The Right to Information Act, 2005 replaced the erstwhile **Freedom of Information Act, 2002**. It operationalises **Article 19(1)(a)** (Freedom of Speech) and **Article 21** (Right to Life). Any citizen of India can file an RTI application for a fee of **₹10**.

**2. Section 4 — Suo Motu Disclosure (1.5 Marks):**
Every public authority (including pollution control boards, factories inspectorate, district authorities) must **voluntarily publish** 17 categories of information, including:
- The particulars of the organization (Section 4(1)(b)(i))
- The powers and duties of officers (Section 4(1)(b)(ii))
- The rules, regulations, and manuals (Section 4(1)(b)(iv))
- **Hazard and safety-related documents** are specifically covered.

**3. Section 6 — Procedure for Application (1.5 Marks):**
- A citizen files an application (in English, Hindi, or the official language of the State).
- The **Public Information Officer (PIO)** must respond within **30 days**.
- For information concerning **life and liberty** of a person (which includes public safety), the response time is **48 hours**.

**4. Section 18 — Penalties for Non-Disclosure (1.5 Marks):**
- The Central Information Commission / State Information Commission can impose a penalty of **₹250/day** on the PIO, subject to a maximum of **₹25,000**.
- Persistent non-compliance → disciplinary action under service rules.

**5. Landmark Cases (1 Mark):**
- *Pune Cantonment Board v. Sanjay Gaikwad* (RTI includes inspection of hazardous goods).
- *Kishan Chand v. State of Rajasthan* (Right to clean environment is enforceable through RTI).

**6. Limitations (0.5 Marks):**
- Section 8 lists exemptions — **strategic, scientific, or economic interests of the State**, and **commercial confidence** of third parties. This is often misused by industries to deny safety information. The Commission balances this against the **"Larger Public Interest"** test.

---

### **OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Describe the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) for classification and labelling of chemicals. Explain its 9 pictograms and 16-section Safety Data Sheet (SDS) format. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the role of Emergency Communication during a major industrial accident. How does the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 protect the public? (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Genesis of GHS (1 Mark):**
The **Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS)** was developed by the **United Nations** and adopted in **2002** at the Johannesburg Summit. It was implemented in India through the **"Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical (Amendment) Rules, 2007"** and aligned with the **Factories Act 1948** amendments.

**2. Objective (0.5 Marks):**
To provide a **single, globally consistent system** for classifying chemicals based on their **health, physical, and environmental hazards**, and to communicate these hazards through **standardized labels and Safety Data Sheets (SDS)**.

**3. The 9 GHS Pictograms (3 Marks):**

| # | Pictogram | Symbol | Hazard Class |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | GHS01 | Exploding Bomb | Explosives, Self-reactives, Organic peroxides |
| 2 | GHS02 | Flame | Flammables, Pyrophorics, Self-heating, Emits flammable gas |
| 3 | GHS03 | Flame over Circle | Oxidizers, Oxidizing gases and liquids |
| 4 | GHS04 | Gas Cylinder | Compressed, Liquefied, Dissolved, Refrigerated gases |
| 5 | GHS05 | Corrosion | Skin corrosion, Eye damage, Corrosive to metals |
| 6 | GHS06 | Skull & Crossbones | Acute toxicity (fatal or toxic) |
| 7 | GHS07 | Exclamation Mark | Irritants, Sensitizers, Narcotic effects |
| 8 | GHS08 | Health Hazard | Carcinogen, Mutagen, Reproductive toxin, Respiratory sensitizer |
| 9 | GHS09 | Environment | Aquatic toxicity, Hazardous to ozone layer |

All pictograms are **red-bordered diamonds** with a **black symbol on white background**.

**4. 16-Section SDS Format (2.5 Marks):**
The SDS must contain exactly **16 sections** in the following order:

1. Identification
2. Hazard(s) Identification
3. Composition / Ingredients
4. First-Aid Measures
5. Fire-Fighting Measures
6. Accidental Release Measures
7. Handling and Storage
8. Exposure Controls / Personal Protection
9. Physical and Chemical Properties
10. Stability and Reactivity
11. Toxicological Information
12. Ecological Information
13. Disposal Considerations
14. Transport Information
15. Regulatory Information
16. Other Information (including date of preparation and revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Mnemonic:** "**I Haz C First F Acc H E P S T E D T R O**" (First letter of each section).

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Definition of Emergency Communication (0.5 Marks):**
Emergency Communication is the **real-time, life-saving information** disseminated during and immediately after a major industrial accident to enable **public protection actions** like evacuation, shelter-in-place, and rescue.

**2. The Communication Cycle (2 Marks):**
$$
\text{Detection} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Activation} \rightarrow \text{Notification} \rightarrow \text{Response} \rightarrow \text{Termination}
$$

- **Detection:** Sensors, control room, or worker.
- **Notification:** Sirens (continuous wail = take shelter; intermittent = all-clear), public address systems, SMS alerts, Doordarshan, All India Radio.
- **Response:** Public must know **three things** — (i) **what happened**, (ii) **where to go**, (iii) **what to do**.

**3. Statutory Anchor — MSIHC Rule 15 and DMA 2005 (1.5 Marks):**
- Under **Rule 15 of MSIHC**, the occupier must inform the public **immediately** on a major accident.
- Under **Section 14 of the Disaster Management Act, 2005**, the **District Magistrate** is the chief coordinator of the off-site emergency response.

**4. Public Liability Insurance Act (PLI Act), 1991 (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The PLI Act, 1991** is the **first Indian law** that specifically deals with **compensation to victims of industrial accidents who are NOT workers** — i.e., the public.

- **Section 2(e)** defines *"hazardous substance"* — same as MSIHC.
- **Section 4** — Mandatory **public liability insurance** by every owner handling a hazardous substance. Minimum cover: **₹5 crores** for death cases.
- **Section 5** — A **Relief Code** must be notified by the State Government specifying the **maximum amount of relief** for death (₹25,000) and injury (₹12,500) — these are **no-fault, immediate relief** amounts, separate from the insurance claim.
- **Section 7** — The owner is **strictly liable** (no proof of negligence required) to pay compensation.
- **Section 10** — The insurer pays compensation as if it were the owner.
- **Section 13** — The **Collector** decides the claim — a quasi-judicial mechanism faster than civil courts.

**5. Connection with Public Information (0.5 Mark):**
The owner must display the **policy number and name of insurer** at the factory gate — a direct obligation to **inform the public** of their financial recourse.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost 3–5 marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing MSDS with SDS** — MSDS is the older (Material Safety Data Sheet) term; SDS is the modern GHS-compliant term. Using both interchangeably loses marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 90-day deadline** — Students often write "immediately" or "within 30 days" for the On-Site Plan filing. The correct statutory period is **90 days from commencement**.
> 
> 3. **Writing "GHS was made by USA"** — Wrong. GHS is by the **United Nations** and is *not* country-specific. It is a recommendation, not a binding treaty.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the 16 sections of SDS** — Examiners allocate **1 mark for the count (16) and 1 mark for naming the first 4 sections**. Skipping these is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 
> 5. **Treating Public Liability Insurance Act as compensation-only** — It also mandates **disclosure of policy** to the public. This dual nature is the KTU-tested angle.
> 
> 6. **Confusing OISD with OSHA** — OISD is the **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (India)**; OSHA is the **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (USA)**. They are NOT the same.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring the Public Hearing stage** — In EIA Notification 2006, **Section 11 Public Hearing is mandatory**. A project without public hearing has **no environment clearance**. This is a high-yield KTU point.

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 20 Critical Things to Remember about "Giving Safety Information to Public":**

1. ✅ The **Constitutional basis** is **Article 21** (Right to Life).
2. ✅ The **primary statute** is the **MSIHC Rules, 1989**, framed under **EPA 1986**.
3. ✅ **Rule 13** → On-Site Emergency Plan; **Rule 14** → Off-Site Emergency Plan; **Rule 15** → Information to Public; **Rule 17** → Penalties.
4. ✅ The **duty-holder** is the **"Occupier"** — the person with ultimate control of the factory.
5. ✅ The **threshold trigger** is **Schedule I & II of MSIHC**.
6. ✅ The **filing deadline** is **90 days** from commencement.
7. ✅ The **review period** is **6-monthly**; the **mock drill** is **annual**.
8. ✅ The **global standard** for chemical classification is **GHS (United Nations)**.
9. ✅ GHS has **9 pictograms**, **2 signal words**, and **16-section SDS**.
10. ✅ GHS pictograms are **red-bordered diamonds** with black symbols.
11. ✅ The **Right to Information Act, 2005** allows any citizen to demand safety records.
12. ✅ The **RTI response time** is **30 days** (48 hours for life and liberty matters).
13. ✅ The **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** mandates insurance for hazardous industries.
14. ✅ The **minimum PLI cover** is **₹5 crores** (centralized; can be revised by State).
15. ✅ PLI is a **no-fault, strict liability** statute — negligence need NOT be proved.
16. ✅ The **Disaster Management Act, 2005** mandates community awareness and mock drills.
17. ✅ The **Factories Act, 1948** Sections 7B, 41, 41A require disclosure of hazardous processes to workers AND neighbours.
18. ✅ The **6 channels** of public communication are: Labels, SDS, Signage, Public Notices, Emergency Plans, Mass Media.
19. ✅ The **9 coordination agencies** include Fire, Police, Medical, SPCB, PESO, AERB, Media, Schools, Panchayat.
20. ✅ The **criminal penalties** for non-disclosure can extend to **7 years imprisonment** and **₹1 lakh + ₹5,000/day** under EPA 1986, plus **3 years and ₹10 lakhs** under MSIHC Rule 17.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Safety information to the public is not charity — it is a **constitutional right** backed by **statutory penalties**. A hazardous industry has the **onus to prove** it has informed, not the public to prove it was kept in the dark.*
